---
title: "**Resident and transient microbial taxa in the gill microbiome of the mussel *Mytilus galloprovincialis***"
documentclass: article
geometry: margin = 1.0in
fontsize: 12 pt
bibliography: ["references.bib", "packages.bib"]
csl: citation_style.csl # Get themes at https://github.com/citation-style-language/styles
link-citations: yes
output:
  bookdown::pdf_document2:
    includes:
      in_header: preamble.tex
      before_body: before_body_manuscript.tex
    keep_tex: true
    latex_engine: pdflatex
    toc: false
    number_sections: false
---

```{r, knitr_settings, eval = TRUE, echo = FALSE, cache = FALSE, results = "hide"}

################################################################################
# Change the default values of chunk options
################################################################################

opts_chunk$set("tidy" = TRUE)
opts_chunk$set("echo" = FALSE)
opts_chunk$set("eval" = TRUE)
opts_chunk$set("warning" = FALSE)
opts_chunk$set("cache" = TRUE)
opts_chunk$set("message" = FALSE)

################################################################################
# Define a custom hook function to format output from inline R expressions
################################################################################

# Define a custom hook function to format output from inline R expressions
# (This enables number formatting during inline R code execution.)
inline_hook <- function(x){
	
  print(x)
  
	if(is.list(x)){
		x <- unlist(x)
	}
  
	if(is.numeric(x)){
		if(abs(x - round(x)) < .Machine$double.eps ^ 0.5){
			paste(format(x, trim = TRUE, big.mark = ',', digits = NULL, scientific = FALSE))
		} else {
			paste(format(x, trim = TRUE, big.mark = ',', digits = 1, nsmall = 1, scientific = FALSE))
		}
	} else {
    	paste(x)
	  }
  }

# Register the custom inline hook
knitr::knit_hooks$set(inline = inline_hook)

################################################################################
# Create and write bib entries for R and currently loaded R packages
################################################################################

# Obtain package names and versions using custom function
packages <- r_package_version(file_path = ".Rprofile")

# Remove package stats
packages <- packages %>%
  filter(name != "stats")

# Create and write bib entries for R and currently loaded R packages
# (add the base package to the list of packages so that R can be cited)
write_bib(x = c("base", packages$name), file = "submission/packages.bib")

# Repair known issues with capitalisation in titles
# Omit capitalisation after colons
system("sed -i 's/^\\(  title = {.*: \\)\\([^ }]*\\)\\(.*\\)/\\1{\\l\\2}\\3/g' submission/packages.bib")
# Capitalise the letter "R" when it indicates the R programming language
system("sed -i 's/^\\(  title = {.*\\) R\\([ }:,]\\)\\(.*\\)/\\1 {R}\\2\\3/g' submission/packages.bib")
# Do not capitalise R package names when they are the first word in the title
# and are followed by a colon
system("sed -i 's/^\\(  title = {\\)\\(knitr\\|rmarkdown\\|bookdown\\|tinytex\\|vegan\\|taxonomizr\\|ggpattern\\|cowplot\\|rlang\\|ggsignif\\|tidyverse\\|legendry\\|cld\\|ggnewscale\\|grid\\|vctrs\\|gtable\\|plyr\\|lazyeval\\|ggforce\\)\\(:.*\\)/\\1{\\2}\\3/g' submission/packages.bib")
# Preserve the capitalisation of specific words
system("sed -i 's/^\\(  title = {.*\\)Markdown\\(.*\\)/\\1{Markdown}\\2/g' submission/packages.bib")
system("sed -i 's/^\\(  title = {.*\\)TeX Live\\(.*\\)/\\1{TeX Live}\\2/g' submission/packages.bib")
system("sed -i 's/^\\(  title = {.*\\)Compact Letter Display\\(.*\\)/\\1{Compact Letter Display}\\2/g' submission/packages.bib")
system("sed -i 's/^\\(  title = {.*\\)colorBrewer\\(.*\\)/\\1ColorBrewer\\2/g' submission/packages.bib")
# When citing book chapters, make the title of the book sentence case
system("sed -i 's/^  booktitle = {Implementing Reproducible Computational Research}/  booktitle = {{Implementing reproducible computational research}}/g' submission/packages.bib")

# Add journal abbreviations
system("sed -i 's/^\\(  journal = {Journal of Open Source Software},\\)/\\1\\
  shortjournal = {J. Open Source Softw.},/g' submission/packages.bib")
system("sed -i 's/^\\(  journal = {Journal of Statistical Software},\\)/\\1\\
  shortjournal = {J. Stat. Softw.},/g' submission/packages.bib")

# Remove urls
system("sed -i '/^  url =/d' submission/packages.bib")

################################################################################
# Retrieve citation keys for the loaded R packages
################################################################################

# Remove packages vegan and tidyverse as they are cited separately
packages <- packages %>%
  filter(name != "vegan") %>%
  filter(name != "tidyverse")

# Retrieve bib entries for the R packages
bib_entries <- write_bib(x = packages$name)

# Unlist the bib entries
bib_entries <- unlist(x = bib_entries)

# Retrieve the citation keys for loaded R packages
citation_keys <- tibble(citation_key = bib_entries) %>%
  # Filter rows containing citation keys
  filter(str_detect(string = citation_key, pattern = "^@")) %>%
  # Extract the citation key from the string
  mutate(citation_key = str_replace(string = citation_key, pattern = "@.*\\{(.*),", replacement = "@\\1"))

```

```{bash cache = FALSE}

# Bash command to replace the language defined as English with undefined
# The CSL capitalises words after a colon for English titles if the following
# text is a complete sentence. To prevent this, the language
# is set to undefined. Ensure all entries in submission/references.bib have
# a langid field set to langid = {English}, langid = {english},
# langid = {en}, or another language. In British English, capitalisation
# occurs only for proper nouns or acronyms after a colon.
sed -i "s/langid = {\\(English\\|english\\|en\\)}/langid = {undefined}/" submission/references.bib

# Bash command to replace the Unicode γ (Greek gamma) wrapped in \mkbibemph{...}
# with LaTeX inline math \gamma
sed -i "s/\\\mkbibemph{γ}/$\\\gamma$/" submission/references.bib

```

```{r, input}

################################################################################
# OTU/sample
################################################################################

# Load OTU/sample data
shared <- read_tsv(file = "data/mothur/raw.trim.contigs.good.unique.good.filter.unique.precluster.denovo.vsearch.pick.pick.opti_mcc.shared")

# Convert the "Group" column to character type
shared <- shared %>%
  mutate(Group = as.character(x = Group))

################################################################################
# Rarefied OTU/sample
################################################################################

# Load the rarefied OTU/sample data
load(file = "results/numerical/rarefied.Rdata")

################################################################################
# Error rate
################################################################################

# Load error rate data
error <- read_tsv("data/mothur/raw.trim.contigs.good.unique.good.filter.unique.precluster.denovo.vsearch.pick.pick.error.summary", na = "")

################################################################################
# Taxonomy
################################################################################

# Load taxonomy data
taxonomy <- read_tsv(file = "data/mothur/raw.trim.contigs.good.unique.good.filter.unique.precluster.denovo.vsearch.silva.wang.tax.summary")

################################################################################
# Metadata
################################################################################

# Load metadata
metadata <- read_tsv(file = "data/raw/metadata.tsv")

################################################################################
# Richness estimators and diversity indices
################################################################################

# Load richness estimators and diversity indices data
load(file = "results/numerical/alpha.Rdata")

################################################################################
# Kruskal-Wallis H test
################################################################################

# Load Kruskal-Wallis H test data
load(file = "results/numerical/kruskal_wallis.Rdata")

################################################################################
# Bray–Curtis dissimilarity coefficients
################################################################################

# Load the Bray–Curtis dissimilarity coefficients
load(file = "results/numerical/beta.Rdata")

```
\vspace{\fill}

Marino Korlević^1\*^, Marsej Markovski^1^, Damir Kapetanović^2^, Irena Vardić Smrzlić^2^, Karla Orlić^2^, Jakša Bolotin^3^, Valter Kožul^3^, Svjetlana Bobanović-Ćolić^3^, Vedrana Nerlović^4^, and Lorena Perić^2\*^

1. Centre for Marine Research, Ruđer Bošković Institute, Croatia
2. Division for Marine and Environmental Research, Ruđer Bošković Institute, Croatia
3. Institute for Marine and Coastal Research, University of Dubrovnik, Croatia
4. Faculty of Marine Sciences, University of Split, Croatia

^\*^To whom correspondence should be addressed:  
Marino Korlević  
G. Paliaga 5, 52210 Rovinj, Croatia  
e-mail: [marino.korlevic\@irb.hr](mailto:marino.korlevic@irb.hr){.email}  
ORCID iD: [0000-0001-5680-3556](https://orcid.org/0000-0001-5680-3556)

Lorena Perić  
Bijenička cesta 54, 10000 Zagreb, Croatia  
e-mail: [lorena.peric@irb.hr](mailto:lorena.peric@irb.hr){.email}  
ORCID iD: [0000-0001-6899-9605](https://orcid.org/0000-0001-6899-9605)

```{=latex}

% Add line numbers
\linenumbers

% Ensure that numbers are typeset in text mode
\sisetup{mode=text}

% Set the size of indentation to 24 pt
\setlength\parindent{24pt}

% Start a new page
\newpage

```

# Abstract

Microorganisms associate with marine bivalves, forming microbial communities collectively known as the bivalve's microbiome. These communities may include resident taxa, associated with the bivalve host throughout its life, and transient taxa, whose presence is temporary and attributed to the passage of, for example, seawater or food through the host. To assess the gill microbiome of the Mediterranean mussel, *Mytilus galloprovincialis* Lamarck, 1819, and its resident and transient taxa, individuals of this important aquaculture species were sampled at two locations on the eastern coast of the Adriatic Sea, Lim Bay and Mali Ston Bay, over one year. The microbiome was characterised using Illumina MiSeq sequencing of the V4 region of the 16S rRNA gene. For comparison, the microbial community in the surrounding seawater and sediment was also assessed at the same time and locations. The structure of the gill microbiome, determined at the OTU level, was distinct from the microbial community in both the surrounding seawater and sediment and did not differ between the two locations. However, it showed differences between months, indicating temporal dynamics. Taxonomic analysis revealed the presence of both resident and transient microbial taxa in the gill microbiome. Members without known relatives within *Endozoicomonadaceae* were present in the gills of mussels from both locations and throughout the study period and appeared to be resident members. In contrast, *Endozoicomonas*, "*Candidatus* Endoecteinascidia", *Vibrio*, and members without known relatives within *Vibrionaceae* were transient, appearing in the gill microbiome only in certain months. Overall, the results of this study show that the gill microbiome of the mussel *Mytilus galloprovincialis* differs from the microbial community in the surrounding seawater and sediment, exhibits temporal dynamics, and contains both resident and transient microbial taxa.

\noindent
**Keywords:** gill microbiome, Mediterranean mussel, *Mytilus galloprovincialis*, aquaculture, Adriatic Sea, 16S rRNA gene sequencing, temporal changes, *Gammaproteobacteria*
\newpage

# Introduction

Marine animals, including bivalves, are associated with various microorganisms such as archaea, bacteria, viruses, protists, and fungi [@Apprill2017; @Salloum2025]. These microbes, collectively known as the animal's microbiome, can reside on or within the animal's body [@Apprill2017; @Petersen2018]. If located inside the body, they may live either outside or within host cells. Notably, microbes within host cells can inhabit specialised cells dedicated to housing symbionts, forming a particularly intimate type of symbiosis [@Petersen2018]. The close relationship between the host and its microbiome can be viewed as a holobiont: an integrated community in which the animal and its symbiotic microbes support each other [@Margulis1991; @Salloum2025]. Furthermore, as suggested by the hologenome concept, the genetic content of the host and the microbes in its microbiome should be considered a single evolutionary unit, meaning that host evolution is influenced by its microbiome [@Bordenstein2015; @Salloum2025].

The bivalve's microbiome may include both resident members, associated with the host throughout its life, and transient members, whose presence is temporary and attributed, for example, to the passage of seawater or food through the animal [@Pierce2018]. Evidence suggests that most microbes in the bivalve's microbiome are resident members [@Unzueta-Martinez2022a]. These microbes may be acquired by the host vertically, transmitted from parent to offspring, horizontally, adopted from the surrounding environment, or through a combination of these processes [@Bright2010; @Petersen2018; @Unzueta-Martinez2022]. Studies have also shown that the microbiome of various bivalve species differs from the microbial community in surrounding seawater or sediment, highlighting the resident nature of most microbiome members [@Akter2023; @Arfken2017; @Lokmer2016; @Musella2020; @Pierce2019; @Unzueta-Martinez2022; @Vezzulli2018]. In addition to differing from the surrounding environment, the bivalve's microbiome also varies between body parts, such as the gut, gills, and haemolymph [@Dube2019; @Lokmer2016; @Musella2020; @Unzueta-Martinez2022; @Vezzulli2018], and is, to some extent and for certain body parts, specific to the host species [@Akter2023; @Gignoux-Wolfsohn2024; @Munoz-Martinez2026; @Pierce2019; @Vezzulli2018]. Although most microbes in the bivalve's microbiome appear to be resident members, a portion is still transient [@Unzueta-Martinez2022a], as suggested by studies reporting temporal changes [@Akter2023; @Nguyen2020; @Pierce2016; @Pierce2019]. The bivalve's microbiome can also differ between geographic locations [@delRio-Lavin2023; @Gignoux-Wolfsohn2024; @Lokmer2016a; @Nguyen2020; @Unzueta-Martinez2022a], indicating that local conditions may also have an influence. Interestingly, the microbiome of bivalves transported from one geographic location to another converges towards that of native bivalves, highlighting the importance of local conditions [@Peruzzo2026; @Sun2025].

Microbes forming the bivalve's microbiome belong to various high-rank bacterial taxa, including the *Pseudomonadota* classes *Alphaproteobacteria* and *Gammaproteobacteria*, *Bacillota*, *Bacteroidota*, *Fusobacteriota*, and *Spirochaetota* [@Gignoux-Wolfsohn2024; @Lasa2019; @Munoz-Martinez2026]. Members of these taxa have also been found in the microbiome of the Mediterranean mussel, *Mytilus galloprovincialis* Lamarck, 1819 [@Auguste2024a; @Akter2023; @Bozcal2020; @delRio-Lavin2023; @Munoz-Martinez2026; @Musella2020; @Palladino2023; @Peruzzo2026; @Schoinas2023; @Vezzulli2018; @Wathsala2021; @Zardinoni2024], an important aquaculture bivalve species widely distributed across the Mediterranean Sea, including the Adriatic Sea [@Gosling2003b; @Gosling2003d; @Hamer2012; @Kijewski2011; @Wenne2022]. Studies focusing on *M. galloprovincialis* have reported patterns of dynamics for this species' microbiome similar to those previously mentioned for the bivalve's microbiome in general. Specifically, differences between the microbiome and the ambient seawater have been reported [@Akter2023; @Musella2020; @Palladino2023; @Vezzulli2018; @Zardinoni2024], as well as microbiome specificity for this host species [@Akter2023; @Munoz-Martinez2026; @Vezzulli2018] and its variation between body parts [@Musella2020; @Vezzulli2018], sampling periods [@Akter2023; @Palladino2023; @Peruzzo2026; @Wathsala2021], and geographic locations [@Palladino2023; @Peruzzo2026; @delRio-Lavin2023].

In general, studies assessing the bivalve's microbiome have focused on gut tissue [@Salloum2025]. Similarly, research on the *M. galloprovincialis* microbiome has usually examined the gut microbiome [@Akter2023; @delRio-Lavin2023; @Palladino2023; @Vezzulli2018; @Wathsala2021] or assessed the microbiome of the whole individual without a tissue-specific sampling approach [@Bozcal2020; @Munoz-Martinez2026; @Schoinas2023; @Peruzzo2026]. Consequently, a limited number of studies have described the temporal and spatial dynamics of the microbiome in other body parts, such as gills and haemolymph [@Auguste2024a; @Sun2025; @Zardinoni2024]. As the mussel *M. galloprovincialis* is a filter-feeding animal [@Gosling2003a], its gills are in constant contact with seawater and may be influenced by sediment resuspension. Therefore, this study focused on assessing the gill microbiome of individuals of this bivalve species, as well as the microbial community in the surrounding seawater and sediment, from two important and geographically distant bivalve aquaculture locations on the eastern coast of the Adriatic Sea, namely Lim Bay and Mali Ston Bay [@BratosCetinic2024], over a period of one year. The study locations are in two legally protected marine reserve areas with distinct hydrological conditions and productivity, typical of the Adriatic Sea biogeographical regions to which they belong [@Bosak2013; @Matek2024; @Silovic2012a; @Skejic2015]. More precisely, the aims of this study were to: (i) compare the mussel gill microbiome with the microbial community in the surrounding seawater and sediment, (ii) determine differences in the gill microbiome between the two aquaculture locations, and (iii) assess changes in the gill microbiome during the one-year study period.

# Methods

## Sampling

This study was conducted at two bivalve aquaculture locations: Lim Bay in the north and Mali Ston Bay in the south of the eastern Adriatic Sea coast (Fig. \@ref(fig:map)). Mussels of the species *M. galloprovincialis*, seawater, and sediment were sampled in July, September, and November 2020 and January, March, and May 2021 (Supplementary Tables \@ref(supp-tab:nseq-notus-seawater-sediment) and \@ref(supp-tab:nseq-notus-gills)), as described in Žurga et al. (2024) [@Zurga2024] and Orlić et al. (2025) [@Orlic2025a]. Briefly, locally produced adult mussels were deployed at an aquaculture site at a depth of 10 \unit{\m} in Lim Bay (\ang{45;7;50}N, \ang{13;41;20}E) and 6 \unit{\m} in Mali Ston Bay (\ang{42;52;16}N, \ang{17;42;12}E), following common farming practices at these locations (Fig. \@ref(fig:map)). At each sampling date, 10 individuals were collected, their gills excised, and snap-frozen in liquid nitrogen. Seawater was collected at a depth close to that at which mussels were deployed, specifically 8 \unit{\m} in Lim Bay and 5 \unit{\m} in Mali Ston Bay, using a Niskin bottle and filtered (0.35–1 \unit{\l}) through 0.2 \unit{\um} membrane filters. Filters were transferred into 2 \unit{\ml} cryotubes and snap-frozen in liquid nitrogen. Sediment was collected using an Ekman grab sampler, transferred into 2 \unit{\ml} cryotubes, and also snap-frozen in liquid nitrogen. Upon arrival at the laboratory, samples of gills and sediment, as well as membrane filters, were stored at \qty{-80}{\degreeCelsius} until further processing. For comparison, in addition to the aquaculture site, seawater and sediment were also sampled at a control site, where no mussels were farmed, in both Lim Bay (\ang{45;7;57}N, \ang{13;38;27}E) and Mali Ston Bay (\ang{42;51;59}N, \ang{17;42;5}E; Fig. \@ref(fig:map)). Seawater from the control site was sampled at the same depth as at the aquaculture site.

```{r, map, out.width = "100%", fig.align = "center", fig.cap = "(ref:map)", , fig.pos = "ht"}

include_graphics(path = "../results/figures/map.jpg")

```
(ref:map) Sampling locations and sites in the Adriatic Sea. At each location, namely Lim Bay and Mali Ston Bay, samples were collected from one aquaculture site and one control site (© OpenStreetMap contributors, \url{https://www.openstreetmap.org/copyright})

## DNA isolation

Total DNA from gills (32–123 \unit{\mg}) and 0.2 \unit{\um} membrane filters was isolated according to Massana et al. (1997) [@Massana1997] with slight modifications. After extraction with organic solvents, 1/10 volume of 3 \unit{\Molar} sodium acetate (pH 5.2) was added. DNA was precipitated by adding 1 volume of cold (\qty{-20}{\degreeCelsius}) isopropanol and incubating the mixture for 1 \unit{\hour} or overnight at \qty{-20}{\degreeCelsius}. The pellet obtained after centrifugation at 20,000 × *g* and \qty{4}{\degreeCelsius} for 20 \unit{\minute} was washed twice with 500 \unit{\ul} of cold (\qty{-20}{\degreeCelsius}) 70\unit{\percent} ethanol. After each wash, the pellet was centrifuged at 20,000 × *g* and \qty{4}{\degreeCelsius} for 5 \unit{\minute}. Air-dried pellets were resuspended in 100 \unit{\ul} of deionised water for DNA isolated from gills and 50 \unit{\ul} for DNA isolated from membrane filters. Total DNA from sediment was isolated as described in Markovski et al. (2022) [@Markovski2022]. This is a modified protocol [@Pjevac2018] of the isolation procedure described in Zhou et al. (1996) [@Zhou1996]. Briefly, 2 \unit{\gram} of sediment was mixed with extraction buffer and proteinase K, and incubated by horizontal shaking at 37 \unit{\degreeCelsius} for 30 \unit{\minute}. After SDS was added, the mixture was incubated again by horizontal shaking at 65 \unit{\degreeCelsius} for 60 \unit{\minute}. Sediment particles were removed by centrifugation and the supernatant was extracted three times with an equal volume of chloroform:isoamyl alcohol (1:1). DNA was precipitated by adding isopropanol and incubating the mixture at 22 \unit{\degreeCelsius} for 60 \unit{\minute}. The pellet obtained by centrifugation was washed twice with cold (\qty{-20}{\degreeCelsius}) 70\unit{\percent} ethanol, air-dried, and resuspended in 100 \unit{\ul} of deionised water.

## Illumina 16S rRNA sequencing

DNA isolated from gills, membrane filters, and sediment was treated with RNase A at a final concentration of 200 \unit{\ug\per\ml} at 37 \unit{\degreeCelsius} for 2 \unit{\hour}. DNA concentration was determined using the Quant-iT PicoGreen dsDNA Assay Kit (Invitrogen, USA) according  to the manufacturer’s instructions. DNA from gills was diluted to 10 \unit{\ng\per\ul}, and DNA from membrane filters and sediment to 1 \unit{\ng\per\ul}. The V4 region of the 16S rRNA gene was amplified using a two-step PCR procedure. In the first PCR, 515F (Parada; 5$'$-GTGYCAGCMGCCGCGGTAA-3$'$) and 806R (Apprill; 5$'$-GGACTACNVGGGTWTCTAAT-3$'$) primers from the Earth Microbiome Project (<https://earthmicrobiome.org/protocols-and-standards/16s>) were used [@Apprill2015; @Caporaso2011; @Caporaso2012; @Parada2016], each with a sequence tag at the 5$'$ end. Four parallel PCRs were performed to amplify the target region from each DNA sample. Each 25 \unit{\ul} reaction contained 1 × Q5 Reaction Buffer, 0.2 \unit{\milli\Molar} dNTP mix, 0.7 \unit{\mg\per\ml} bovine serum albumin (BSA), 0.5 \unit{\micro\Molar} forward and
reverse primers, 0.5 \unit{\U} Q5 High-Fidelity DNA Polymerase (New England Biolabs, USA), and 50 \unit{\ng} DNA template from gills or 5 \unit{\ng} from membrane filters and sediment. The cycling conditions were: initial denaturation at 98 \unit{\degreeCelsius} for 30 \unit{\s}; 25 cycles of denaturation at 98 \unit{\degreeCelsius} for 10 \unit{\s}, annealing at 50 \unit{\degreeCelsius} for 30 \unit{\s}, and elongation at 72 \unit{\degreeCelsius} for 5 \unit{\s} for reactions with DNA from gills or 30 \unit{\s} for reactions with DNA from membrane filters and sediment. PCR cycling ended with a final elongation at 72 \unit{\degreeCelsius} for 2 \unit{\minute}. The higher amount of DNA template (50 \unit{\ng}) and shorter elongation time (5 \unit{\s}) used for amplifying the target region from DNA isolated from gills were optimised to reduce amplification of non-specific fragments. The four parallel reactions were pooled, and PCR products were purified using the GeneJET PCR Purification Kit (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA) according to the manufacturer’s instructions. The protocol without isopropanol addition was followed to avoid co-purification of primer-dimers. The column was eluted in 30 \unit{\ul} deionised water.

Purified PCR products were sent to IMGM Laboratories, Martinsried, Germany, for Illumina MiSeq sequencing (2 × 250 \unit{\bp}). Before sequencing, the second PCR in the two-step PCR procedure was performed using primers that bind to the sequence tag added during the first PCR. Adapter and sample-specific index sequences were also incorporated in the second PCR by including these sequences at the 5$'$ end of the primers used. This PCR was performed for eight cycles. In addition to the gill, seawater, and sediment samples, two positive and two negative controls were sequenced. Each positive control consisted of four parallel PCRs using genomic DNA from a mock community composed of evenly mixed DNA from 20 bacterial strains (ATCC MSA-1002, ATCC, USA) as the DNA template. Each negative control comprised four parallel PCRs without a DNA template. One positive and one negative control were processed according to the PCR procedure for gill samples, and one positive and one negative control according to the PCR procedure for seawater and sediment samples.

## Sequence and data analysis

Sequences obtained were analysed using mothur (version `r system("code/mothur/mothur --version", intern = TRUE)[2] %>% str_extract(pattern = "[0-9.()]+")`) [@Schloss2009] following the MiSeq Standard Operating Procedure (MiSeq SOP\; <https://mothur.org/wiki/miseq_sop>) [@Kozich2013] and recommendations from the Riffomonas project to enhance data reproducibility (<https://riffomonas.org>). For alignment and classification, the SILVA SSU Ref NR 99 database (release 138.2; <https://www.arb-silva.de>) was used [@Quast2013; @Yilmaz2014]. Sequences were clustered into operational taxonomic units (OTUs) using a  97\unit{\percent} similarity threshold.

Data processing and visualisation were performed using R (version `r R.version$version.string %>% str_extract(pattern = "[0-9]+\\.[0-9]+\\.[0-9]+")`) [@R-base] with the packages vegan (version `r as.character(packageVersion(pkg = "vegan")) %>% str_replace(pattern = "\\.([^.]+$)", replacement = "-\\1")`) [@R-vegan] and tidyverse (version `r as.character(packageVersion(pkg = "tidyverse"))`) [@R-tidyverse; @tidyverse2019], as well as several other packages [`r deframe(x = citation_keys) %>% paste0(collapse = "; ")`]. The observed number of OTUs, Chao1, ACE, exponential of the Shannon diversity index, and Inverse Simpson diversity index were calculated after normalisation to the minimum number of sequences per sample using vegan’s function `rrarefy` to account for differences in sequencing depth [@R-vegan]. Richness estimators Chao1 and ACE were obtained using vegan's function `estimateR`, while the Shannon and Inverse Simpson diversity indices were calculated using vegan's function `diversity` [@R-vegan]. To express both diversity indices as the effective number of OTUs, the exponential of the Shannon diversity index was calculated [@Jost2006]. Variation in microbial community structure was assessed on OTU abundances by pooling sequences from each environment, site, location, or month and calculating the Bray–Curtis dissimilarity coefficient using vegan’s function `vegdist` after normalising the newly created pools to the minimum number of sequences per pool with vegan’s function `rrarefy` [@Borcard2018; @Legendre2012; @R-vegan]. Differences between these communities from distinct environments, sites, locations, and months were tested by performing Analysis of Similarities (ANOSIM) using vegan’s function `anosim` with 999 permutations [@R-vegan]. Differences between richness estimators, diversity indices, and proportions of taxa were tested using the Mann–Whitney *U* test with the function `wilcox.test` when two groups were compared, or the Kruskal–Wallis *H* test with the function `kruskal.test`, followed by pairwise comparison with the Mann–Whitney *U* test using the function `pairwise.wilcox.test`, when more than two groups were compared [@R-base]. The Bonferroni correction was applied to address the multiple comparisons problem.

```{r, methods-sequence-otu-number}

# Calculate the total number of sequences
sequence_number <- shared %>%
  # Select columns that start with "Otu"
  select(starts_with(match = "Otu")) %>%
  # Sum the sequences and convert to millions
  sum() / 1e6

# Calculate the total number of OTUs
otu_number <- shared %>%
  # Select columns that start with "Otu"
  select(starts_with(match = "Otu")) %>%
  # Extract the names of the selected columns
  colnames() %>%
  # Calculate the number of columns that
  # start with "Otu"
  length()

# Calculate the number of sequences and OTUs in each sample
nseq_notus <- shared %>%
  # Sum the number of sequences in each sample
  mutate(nseq = rowSums(x = select(., starts_with(match = "Otu")))) %>%
  # Sum the number of OTUs in each sample
  mutate(notus = rowSums(x = select(., starts_with(match = "Otu")) != 0))

```

```{r, methods-rarefied-sequence-otu-number}

# Calculate the total number of OTUs from a randomly
# rarefied version of the community data
rarefied_otu_number <- rarefied %>%
  # Select columns that start with "Otu"
  select(starts_with(match = "Otu")) %>%
  # Extract the names of the selected columns
  colnames() %>%
  # Calculate the number of columns that
  # start with "Otu"
  length()

# Calculate the number of sequences and OTUs in each sample
# using a randomly rarefied version of the community data
rarefied_nseq_notus <- rarefied %>%
  # Sum the number of sequences in each sample
  mutate(nseq = rowSums(x = select(., starts_with(match = "Otu")))) %>%
  # Sum the number of OTUs in each sample
  mutate(notus = rowSums(x = select(., starts_with(match = "Otu")) != 0))

```

```{r, methods-error}

# Calculate the sequencing error rate based on the ATCC MSA-1002 mock community
error_rate <- error %>%
  # Filter sequences that are not chimeric (numparents == 1)
  filter(numparents == 1) %>%
  # Calculate the total number of mismatches
  mutate(mismatches_weight = weight * mismatches) %>%
  # Calculate the total number of bases
  mutate(total_weight = weight * total) %>%
  # Calculate the sequencing error rate as the sum of mismatches divided by the
  # sum of bases in the query, multiplied by 100 to get a percentage
  summarise(error_rate = sum(mismatches_weight) / sum(total_weight) * 100)

# Calculate the number of sequences in negative controls
sequence_nc <- taxonomy %>%
  # Filter the row containing the total number of sequences
  filter(taxon == "Root") %>%
  # Select the negative controls
  select(NC1, NC2)

```
From six sampling months, two locations (Lim Bay and Mali Ston Bay), and two sites (aquaculture and control), `r 6 * 2 * 2` seawater and `r 6 * 2 * 2` sediment samples were processed. Additionally, from 10 individuals collected at the aquaculture site during the same months and locations, `r 6 * 2 * 1 * 10` gill samples were processed. This resulted in a total of `r 6 * 2 * 2 * 2 + 6 * 2 * 1 * 10` samples, of which `r metadata %>% filter(!(id %in% c("PC1", "PC2", "NC1", "NC2"))) %>% summarise(n_samples = n())` yielded sufficient sequences for further analysis (Supplementary Tables \@ref(supp-tab:nseq-notus-seawater-sediment) and \@ref(supp-tab:nseq-notus-gills)). From these samples, a total of `r sequence_number` million sequences were obtained after quality curation and exclusion of sequences without known relatives (no relative sequences), as well as eukaryotic, chloroplast, and mitochondrial sequences. The number of sequences per sample ranged from `r min(nseq_notus$nseq)` to `r max(nseq_notus$nseq)` (Supplementary Tables \@ref(supp-tab:nseq-notus-seawater-sediment) and \@ref(supp-tab:nseq-notus-gills)). These sequences were clustered into a total of `r otu_number` different OTUs. For seawater and sediment samples, as well as for gill samples with a high number of OTUs, the rarefaction curves did not level off, even with the highest sequencing effort, as is commonly observed in high-throughput 16S rRNA amplicon sequencing approaches (Supplementary Figs. \@ref(supp-fig:rarefaction-a) and \@ref(supp-fig:rarefaction-b)). However, for gill samples with a low number of OTUs, the rarefaction curves did level off. Normalisation to the minimum number of sequences per sample (`r min(nseq_notus$nseq)` sequences) resulted in `r rarefied_otu_number` different OTUs, ranging from `r min(rarefied_nseq_notus$notus)` to `r max(rarefied_nseq_notus$notus)` OTUs per sample. Based on the positive controls (mock community ATCC MSA-1002), a sequencing error rate of `r error_rate$error_rate`\unit{\percent} was calculated, which is comparable to previously reported values for next-generation 16S rRNA amplicon sequencing [@Korlevic2021a; @Kozich2013; @Markovski2022; @Schloss2016]. In addition, the two negative controls processed with the samples yielded `r min(sequence_nc)` and `r max(sequence_nc)` sequences after sequence quality curation.
\newpage

# Results

```{r, results-alpha-observed-otus}

# Extract the values of the observed number of OTUs for reporting
alpha_otu_seawater_aq <- alpha %>%
  filter(parameter == "S.obs") %>%
  filter(environment == "Seawater") %>%
  filter(site == "Aquaculture")
alpha_otu_seawater_ctrl <- alpha %>%
  filter(parameter == "S.obs") %>%
  filter(environment == "Seawater") %>%
  filter(site == "Control")
alpha_otu_sediment_aq <- alpha %>%
  filter(parameter == "S.obs") %>%
  filter(environment == "Sediment") %>%
  filter(site == "Aquaculture")
alpha_otu_sediment_ctrl <- alpha %>%
  filter(parameter == "S.obs") %>%
  filter(environment == "Sediment") %>%
  filter(site == "Control")
alpha_otu_gills_aq <- alpha %>%
  filter(parameter == "S.obs") %>%
  filter(environment == "Gills") %>%
  filter(site == "Aquaculture")

```

```{r, results-alpha-locations}

# Extract the values of the Inverse Simpson diversity index of sediment
# samples for reporting
alpha_invs_sediment_lim <- alpha %>%
  filter(parameter == "invsimpson") %>%
  filter(environment == "Sediment") %>%
  filter(location == "Lim Bay")
alpha_invs_sediment_mali_ston <- alpha %>%
  filter(parameter == "invsimpson") %>%
  filter(environment == "Sediment") %>%
  filter(location == "Mali Ston Bay")

# Define the object name for storing statistical results
wilcox_location <- NULL

# Perform the Mann-Whitney U test for richness estimators and diversity indices
# to test differences between locations for each environment
for (i in c("Seawater", "Sediment", "Gills")) {
  
  # Filter each environment
  data <- alpha %>%
    filter(environment == i)
  
  for (j in c("S.obs", "S.chao1", "S.ACE", "eshannon", "invsimpson")) {
    
    # Filter each richness estimator and diversity index
    # for samples from Lim Bay
    data_x <- data %>%
      filter(parameter == j) %>%
      filter(location == "Lim Bay")
    # Filter each richness estimator and diversity index
    # for samples from Mali Ston Bay
    data_y <- data %>%
      filter(parameter == j) %>%
      filter(location == "Mali Ston Bay")
    
    # Perform the Mann-Whitney U test
    test <- wilcox.test(x = data_x$value, y = data_y$value,
                        alternative = "two.sided", paired = FALSE)
  
    # Store the statistical results in a tibble
    test_data <- tribble(
      # Define the column names for the tibble
      ~ environment, ~ parameter, ~ n_x, ~ n_y, ~ W, ~ p,
      # Add rows to the tibble with the Mann-Whitney U test results
      i, j, length(data_x$value), length(data_y$value),
      test$statistic, test$p.value)
  
    # Combine the statistical results of each richness estimator
    # and diversity index in a tibble
    wilcox_location <- bind_rows(wilcox_location,
                                 test_data)
    
  }
}

# Extract statistical values for reporting 
wilcox_location <- wilcox_location %>%
  # Filter the row containing statistical results for the sediment
  # microbial community and the Inverse Simpson diversity index
  filter(environment == "Sediment", parameter == "invsimpson")

```

```{r, results-alpha-gills-months}

# Define the object name for storing statistical results
kruskal_wallis_gills_months <- NULL

# Perform the Kruskal-Wallis H test for richness estimators and
# diversity indices across different months for gill samples
for (i in c("S.obs", "S.chao1", "S.ACE", "eshannon", "invsimpson")) {
  
  # Prepare data for testing
  data <- alpha %>%
    # Filter gill samples
    filter(environment == "Gills") %>%
    # Filter each richness estimator and diversity index
    filter(parameter == i) %>%
    # Define testing groups
    mutate(group = interaction(month, year, sep = "_"))
  
  # Perform the Kruskal-Wallis H test
  kruskal <- kruskal.test(formula = value ~ group,
                          data = data)
  
  # Perform the pairwise Mann-Whitney U test
  pairwise_wilcox <- pairwise.wilcox.test(
    x = data$value, g = data$group,
    p.adjust.method = "bonferroni")
  
  # Convert the results of the pairwise Mann-Whitney U test
  # to a tibble
  pairwise_wilcox_tibble <- pairwise_wilcox$p.value %>%
    # Convert the results to a tibble
    as_tibble(.name_repair = "check_unique", rownames = NA) %>%
    # Transfer the testing group names from row names to a column
    rownames_to_column("group")
    
  # Store the statistical results in a tibble
  test_data <- tribble(
    # Define the column names for the tibble
    ~ parameter, ~ n, ~ kw_H, ~ kw_df, ~ kw_p, ~ mw_p, 
    # Add rows to the tibble with the Kruskal-Wallis H test and
    # pairwise Mann-Whitney U test results
    i, count(x = data, group),
    kruskal$statistic, kruskal$parameter, kruskal$p.value,
    pairwise_wilcox_tibble)
  
  # Combine the statistical results of each richness estimator
  # and diversity index in a tibble
  kruskal_wallis_gills_months <- bind_rows(kruskal_wallis_gills_months,
                                           test_data)
  
}

# Extract statistical values for reporting 
kruskal_wallis_gills_months <- kruskal_wallis_gills_months %>%
  # Filter rows containing statistical results on richness estimators
  # Chao1 and ACE
  filter(parameter == "S.chao1" | parameter == "S.ACE") %>%
  # Expand the list-column "mw_p" containing tibbles into rows and columns
  unnest(cols = mw_p)

```
To assess the richness and diversity of microbial communities, the observed number of OTUs, the richness estimators Chao1 and ACE, the exponential of the Shannon diversity index, and the Inverse Simpson diversity index were calculated at the OTU level for the sampled environments (seawater, sediment, and the gills of *M. galloprovincialis*) at both aquaculture and control sites (Fig. \@ref(fig:alpha)). Statistically significant changes were found for all parameters (Kruskal–Wallis *H* test, all *p* `r format_p_values(x = max(kruskal_wallis$kruskal_wallis_tibble$kw_p), space = TRUE)`; Supplementary Table \@ref(supp-tab:kruskal-wallis)). The sediment microbial community had the highest observed number of OTUs (aquaculture, `r min(alpha_otu_sediment_aq$value)`–`r max(alpha_otu_sediment_aq$value)` OTUs; control, `r min(alpha_otu_sediment_ctrl$value)`–`r max(alpha_otu_sediment_ctrl$value)` OTUs), followed by the seawater community (aquaculture, `r min(alpha_otu_seawater_aq$value)`–`r max(alpha_otu_seawater_aq$value)` OTUs; control, `r min(alpha_otu_seawater_ctrl$value)`–`r max(alpha_otu_seawater_ctrl$value)` OTUs), and the gill microbiome (aquaculture, `r min(alpha_otu_gills_aq$value)`–`r max(alpha_otu_gills_aq$value)` OTUs). No significant differences in the observed number of OTUs were found between the aquaculture and control sites for the sediment and seawater communities (Fig. \@ref(fig:alpha) and Supplementary Table \@ref(supp-tab:kruskal-wallis)). The same pattern was observed for the exponential of the Shannon diversity index and the Inverse Simpson diversity index. However, when the richness estimators Chao1 and ACE were calculated, differences between the gill microbiome and seawater microbial communities at the aquaculture and control sites were no longer observed (Fig. \@ref(fig:alpha) and Supplementary Table \@ref(supp-tab:kruskal-wallis)). Additionally, neither parameter showed differences between the sampling locations, except for the Inverse Simpson diversity index of sediment communities, which showed higher values in Mali Ston Bay (`r min(alpha_invs_sediment_mali_ston$value)`–`r max(alpha_invs_sediment_mali_ston$value)` OTUs) than in Lim Bay (`r min(alpha_invs_sediment_lim$value)`–`r max(alpha_invs_sediment_lim$value)` OTUs; Mann–Whitney *U* test, *p* `r format_p_values(x = wilcox_location$p, space = TRUE)`). Furthermore, for the gill microbiome, only the richness estimators Chao1 and ACE showed differences across sampling months (Kruskal–Wallis *H* test, both *p* `r format_p_values(x = max(kruskal_wallis_gills_months$kw_p), space = TRUE)`). However, the only significant difference found was between January and March 2021 (pairwise Mann–Whitney *U* test, both *p* `r filter(.data = kruskal_wallis_gills_months, group == "March_2021")$January_2021 %>% max() %>% format_p_values(space = TRUE)`).

```{r, alpha, out.width = "90%", fig.align = "center", fig.cap = "(ref:alpha)", fig.pos = "ht!"}

include_graphics(path = "../results/figures/alpha.jpg")

```
(ref:alpha) The observed number of OTUs, Chao1, ACE, exponential of the Shannon diversity index, and Inverse Simpson diversity index of microbial communities from different environments and sites. Dashed boxes indicate groups of samples from the control site, while non-dashed areas represent groups of samples from the aquaculture site. Groups that do not share a letter are significantly different (*p* < 0.05)

```{r, results-beta-environments-sites}

# Extract the values of the Bray–Curtis dissimilarity coefficient
# for reporting
bray_seawater_aq_ctrl <- bray %>%
  filter(y_strip == "Environments and Sites") %>%
  filter((columns_inside == "Seawater" & columns_outside == "Aquaculture") &
         (rows_inside == "Seawater" & rows_outside == "Control"))
bray_sediment_aq_ctrl <- bray %>%
  filter(y_strip == "Environments and Sites") %>%
  filter((columns_inside == "Sediment" & columns_outside == "Aquaculture") &
         (rows_inside == "Sediment" & rows_outside == "Control"))
bray_other <- bray %>%
  filter(y_strip == "Environments and Sites") %>%
  filter(!((columns_inside == "Seawater" & columns_outside == "Aquaculture") &
           (rows_inside == "Seawater" & rows_outside == "Control")) &
         !((columns_inside == "Sediment" & columns_outside == "Aquaculture") &
           (rows_inside == "Sediment" & rows_outside == "Control")))

################################################################################
# Calculate ANOSIM for the overall microbial community using environments
# as a factor for grouping
################################################################################

# Customise metadata and join metadata with
# rarefied OTU/sample data
metadata_rarefied <- metadata %>%
  # Customise metadata using a custom function
  format_labels() %>%
  # Join metadata with rarefied OTU/sample data
  right_join(y = rarefied, by = c("id" = "id"))

# Define ANOSIM analysis groups
groups <- metadata_rarefied %>%
  # Group samples based on the sampled
  # environment
  mutate(groups = environment) %>%
  # Select the created column "groups"
  select(groups) %>%
  # Convert the tibble to a vector
  deframe()

# Select OTU abundance data
metadata_rarefied <- metadata_rarefied %>%
  # Select columns containing OTU abundance data
  select(id, starts_with(match = "Otu")) %>%
  # Add sample IDs to row names
  column_to_rownames("id")

# Calculate ANOSIM
anosim_environments <- anosim(x = metadata_rarefied, grouping = groups,
                              permutations = 999, distance = "bray")

################################################################################
# Calculate ANOSIM for the seawater and sediment communities
# using sites as a factor for grouping
################################################################################

# Calculate the Bray–Curtis dissimilarity coefficients between sampling
# sites using the custom function
bray_seawater_sites <- custom_bray(shared = shared,
                                   metadata = metadata,
                                   filter_environment = "Seawater",
                                   group_by = "site")
bray_sediment_sites <- custom_bray(shared = shared,
                                   metadata = metadata,
                                   filter_environment = "Sediment",
                                   group_by = "site")

# Calculate ANOSIM
anosim_seawater_sites <- anosim(
  x = select(.data = bray_seawater_sites$rarefied_data,
             starts_with(match = "Otu")),
  grouping = select(.data = bray_seawater_sites$rarefied_data,
                    site)$site, permutations = 999, distance = "bray")
anosim_sediment_sites <- anosim(
  x = select(.data = bray_sediment_sites$rarefied_data,
             starts_with(match = "Otu")),
  grouping = select(.data = bray_sediment_sites$rarefied_data,
                    site)$site, permutations = 999, distance = "bray")

```

```{r, results-beta-locations}

# Calculate the Bray–Curtis dissimilarity coefficients between sampling
# locations using the custom function
bray_seawater_locations <- custom_bray(shared = shared,
                                       metadata = metadata,
                                       filter_environment = "Seawater",
                                       group_by = "location")
bray_sediment_locations <- custom_bray(shared = shared,
                                       metadata = metadata,
                                       filter_environment = "Sediment",
                                       group_by = "location")
bray_gills_locations <- custom_bray(shared = shared,
                                    metadata = metadata,
                                    filter_environment = "Gills",
                                    group_by = "location")

# Calculate ANOSIM for each environment using locations as a
# factor for grouping
anosim_seawater_locations <- anosim(
  x = select(.data = bray_seawater_locations$rarefied_data,
             starts_with(match = "Otu")),
  grouping = select(.data = bray_seawater_locations$rarefied_data,
                    location)$location, permutations = 999, distance = "bray")
anosim_sediment_locations <- anosim(
  x = select(.data = bray_sediment_locations$rarefied_data,
             starts_with(match = "Otu")),
  grouping = select(.data = bray_sediment_locations$rarefied_data,
                    location)$location, permutations = 999, distance = "bray")
anosim_gills_locations <- anosim(
  x = select(.data = bray_gills_locations$rarefied_data,
             starts_with(match = "Otu")),
  grouping = select(.data = bray_gills_locations$rarefied_data,
                    location)$location, permutations = 999, distance = "bray")

# Extract the ANOSIM R value containing the minus sign for reporting
# (omits the inline usage of code containing $ signs and embraced by $ signs;
# $ signs are required to properly render the minus sign in the output;
# if code containing $ signs and embraced by $ signs is used, unusual
# highlighting in RStudio is obtained)
anosim_seawater_sites_statistic <- custom_round(
  x = anosim_seawater_sites$statistic)

```

```{r, results-beta-gills-months}

# Extract the values of the Bray–Curtis dissimilarity coefficient
# for reporting
bray_gills_sep_2020_jan_2021 <- bray %>%
  filter(y_strip == "Gill Microbiome—Sampling Months") %>%
  filter(((columns_inside == "September" & columns_outside == "2020") &
         (rows_inside == "November" & rows_outside == "2020")) |
         ((columns_inside == "September" & columns_outside == "2020") &
         (rows_inside == "January" & rows_outside == "2021")) |
         ((columns_inside == "November" & columns_outside == "2020") &
         (rows_inside == "January" & rows_outside == "2021")))
bray_gills_mar_2021_may_2021 <- bray %>%
  filter(y_strip == "Gill Microbiome—Sampling Months") %>%
  filter((columns_inside == "March" & columns_outside == "2021") &
         (rows_inside == "May" & rows_outside == "2021"))
bray_gills_other <- bray %>%
  filter(y_strip == "Gill Microbiome—Sampling Months") %>%
  filter(!(((columns_inside == "September" & columns_outside == "2020") &
            (rows_inside == "November" & rows_outside == "2020")) |
          ((columns_inside == "September" & columns_outside == "2020") &
           (rows_inside == "January" & rows_outside == "2021")) |
          ((columns_inside == "November" & columns_outside == "2020") &
           (rows_inside == "January" & rows_outside == "2021")) |
          ((columns_inside == "March" & columns_outside == "2021") &
           (rows_inside == "May" & rows_outside == "2021"))))

################################################################################
# Calculate ANOSIM for the gill microbiomes using specified periods as a
# factor for grouping
################################################################################

# Calculate the Bray–Curtis dissimilarity coefficients between sampling months
# using the custom function
bray_gills_months <- custom_bray(shared = shared,
                                 metadata = metadata,
                                 filter_environment = "Gills",
                                 group_by = c("month", "year"))

# Define ANOSIM analysis groups
groups <- bray_gills_months$rarefied_data %>%
  # Group samples based on the specified period
  mutate(groups = case_when((month == "July" & year == "2020") ~
                              "July_2020",
                            (month == "September" & year == "2020") |
                            (month == "November" & year == "2020") |
                            (month == "January" & year == "2021") ~
                              "September_2020–January_2021",
                            (month == "March" & year == "2021") |
                            (month == "May" & year == "2021") ~
                              "March_2021–May_2021",
                            TRUE ~ month)) %>%
  # Select the created column "groups"
  select(groups) %>%
  # Convert the tibble to a vector
  deframe()

# Calculate ANOSIM
anosim_gills_periods <- anosim(x = select(.data = bray_gills_months$rarefied_data,
                                          starts_with(match = "Otu")),
                               grouping = groups,
                               permutations = 999, distance = "bray")

```
To assess variation in the structure of microbial communities between the sampled environments and sites, Bray–Curtis dissimilarity coefficients based on OTU abundances were calculated (Fig. \@ref(fig:beta)). Except for the seawater (Bray–Curtis dissimilarity, `r custom_round(x = bray_seawater_aq_ctrl$bray)`) and sediment (Bray–Curtis dissimilarity, `r custom_round(x = bray_sediment_aq_ctrl$bray)`) communities, which showed lower variation between the aquaculture and control sites, all other dissimilarities between sampled environments and sites were generally high (Bray–Curtis dissimilarity, `r custom_round(x = min(bray_other$bray))`–`r custom_round(x = max(bray_other$bray))`). The pronounced distinction of microbial communities from different environments was also supported by ANOSIM (R = `r custom_round(x = anosim_environments$statistic)`, *p* `r format_p_values(x = anosim_environments$signif, space = TRUE)`). In contrast, ANOSIM did not support differentiation of the seawater (R = $`r anosim_seawater_sites_statistic`$, *p* = `r format_p_values(x = anosim_seawater_sites$signif, space = TRUE)`) and sediment communities (R = `r custom_round(x = anosim_sediment_sites$statistic)`, *p* = `r format_p_values(x = anosim_sediment_sites$signif, space = TRUE)`) between the aquaculture and control sites. When communities from different locations were compared, gill microbiomes showed the lowest degree of variation between sampling locations (Bray–Curtis dissimilarity, `r custom_round(x = bray_gills_locations$bray_value$bray)`), followed by the seawater (Bray–Curtis dissimilarity, `r custom_round(x = bray_seawater_locations$bray_value$bray)`) and sediment (Bray–Curtis dissimilarity, `r custom_round(x = bray_sediment_locations$bray_value$bray)`) microbial communities. ANOSIM supported differentiation between locations, with some community overlap, only for sediment communities (R = `r custom_round(x = anosim_sediment_locations$statistic)`, *p* `r format_p_values(x = anosim_sediment_locations$signif, space = TRUE)`). When sampling months of the gill microbiome were compared, lower variation was observed between microbiomes from September 2020 to January 2021 (Bray–Curtis dissimilarity, `r custom_round(x = min(bray_gills_sep_2020_jan_2021$bray))`–`r custom_round(x = max(bray_gills_sep_2020_jan_2021$bray))`) and from March to May 2021 (Bray–Curtis dissimilarity, `r custom_round(x = bray_gills_mar_2021_may_2021$bray)`) compared to other dissimilarities between sampling months (Bray–Curtis dissimilarity, `r custom_round(x = min(bray_gills_other$bray))`–`r custom_round(x = max(bray_gills_other$bray))`; Fig. \@ref(fig:beta)). The separation of gill microbiomes from July 2020, September 2020 to January 2021, and March to May 2021 was also supported by ANOSIM, although with substantial community overlap (R = `r custom_round(x = anosim_gills_periods$statistic)`, *p* `r format_p_values(x = anosim_gills_periods$signif, space = TRUE)`).

```{r, beta, out.width = "100%", fig.align = "center", fig.cap = "(ref:beta)", fig.pos = "t!"}

include_graphics(path = "../results/figures/beta.jpg")

```
(ref:beta) Bray–Curtis dissimilarity coefficients calculated from the abundances of OTUs between microbial communities from different environments and sites, and between microbial communities from the gills of the mussel *M. galloprovincialis* (gill microbiome) sampled in different months

```{r, taxonomy-environments-sites, out.width = "100%", fig.align = "center", fig.cap = "(ref:taxonomy-environments-sites)", fig.pos = "t!"}

include_graphics(path = "../results/figures/taxonomy_environments_sites.jpg")

```
(ref:taxonomy-environments-sites) Taxonomic classification and average relative contribution of the most abundant (\qty{>=3}{\percent}) bacterial and archaeal sequences in different sampled environments and at each site. "No Relative" indicates sequences that could not be classified into any taxon, while "NR" (No Relative) next to the name of a taxonomic group refers to sequences that could not be assigned to any known taxon within that group

```{r, results-taxonomy-environments-sites}

# Clean taxonomy data using the custom function
taxonomy_environments_sites <- custom_clean_taxonomy(
  input = taxonomy,
  control = c("PC1", "PC2", "NC1", "NC2"),
  remove_chloroplast = TRUE)

# Structure the taxonomy data using the custom function
taxonomy_environments_sites <- custom_structure_taxonomy(
  input = taxonomy_environments_sites,
  metadata = metadata %>%
    # Customise metadata using the custom function
    format_labels() %>%
    # Remove positive and negative controls from metadata
    filter(!(id %in% c("PC1", "PC2", "NC1", "NC2"))),
  taxa_colour = NULL,
  select_environment = NULL,
  select_taxlevel = 2,
  threshold = 3,
  include_chloroplast = FALSE,
  split_pseudomonadota = TRUE)

# Group the samples by "environment", "site", and
# "taxon" and summarise the data by calculating the
# mean, standard deviation, and minimum and maximum values
taxonomy_environments_sites <-
  taxonomy_environments_sites$longer_metadata_taxonomy %>%
  group_by(environment, site, taxon) %>%
  summarise(mean = mean(proportion),
            sd = sd(proportion),
            min = min(proportion),
            max = max(proportion),
            .groups = "drop")

# Extract the proportions of the most abundant taxa for reporting
taxonomy_seawater_aq <- taxonomy_environments_sites %>%
  filter(environment == "Seawater") %>%
  filter(site == "Aquaculture") %>%
  arrange(desc(x = mean))
taxonomy_seawater_ctrl <- taxonomy_environments_sites %>%
  filter(environment == "Seawater") %>%
  filter(site == "Control") %>%
  arrange(desc(x = mean))
taxonomy_sediment_aq <- taxonomy_environments_sites %>%
  filter(environment == "Sediment") %>%
  filter(site == "Aquaculture") %>%
  arrange(desc(x = mean))
taxonomy_sediment_ctrl <- taxonomy_environments_sites %>%
  filter(environment == "Sediment") %>%
  filter(site == "Control") %>%
  arrange(desc(x = mean))
taxonomy_gills_aq <- taxonomy_environments_sites %>%
  filter(environment == "Gills") %>%
  filter(site == "Aquaculture") %>%
  arrange(desc(x = mean))

```
Differences in community structure between environments and similarities between sites were also evident in the taxonomic composition of the microbial communities (Fig. \@ref(fig:taxonomy-environments-sites)). There were no notable differences between the aquaculture and control sites for the sediment and seawater communities. The seawater microbial community was mainly composed of the bacterial taxa *Alphaproteobacteria* (aquaculture, `r custom_min_max(input = taxonomy_seawater_aq, taxon = "Alphaproteobacteria")`\unit{\percent}; control, `r custom_min_max(input = taxonomy_seawater_ctrl, taxon = "Alphaproteobacteria")`\unit{\percent}), *Gammaproteobacteria* (aquaculture, `r custom_min_max(input = taxonomy_seawater_aq, taxon = "Gammaproteobacteria")`\unit{\percent}; control, `r custom_min_max(input = taxonomy_seawater_ctrl, taxon = "Gammaproteobacteria")`\unit{\percent}), *Bacteroidota* (aquaculture, `r custom_min_max(input = taxonomy_seawater_aq, taxon = "Bacteroidota")`\unit{\percent}; control, `r custom_min_max(input = taxonomy_seawater_ctrl, taxon = "Bacteroidota")`\unit{\percent}), *Cyanobacteriota* (aquaculture, `r custom_min_max(input = taxonomy_seawater_aq, taxon = "Cyanobacteriota")`\unit{\percent}; control, `r custom_min_max(input = taxonomy_seawater_ctrl, taxon = "Cyanobacteriota")`\unit{\percent}), *Verrucomicrobiota* (aquaculture, `r custom_min_max(input = taxonomy_seawater_aq, taxon = "Verrucomicrobiota")`\unit{\percent}; control, `r custom_min_max(input = taxonomy_seawater_ctrl, taxon = "Verrucomicrobiota")`\unit{\percent}), and the archaeal phylum *Thermoplasmatota* (aquaculture, `r custom_min_max(input = taxonomy_seawater_aq, taxon = "Thermoplasmatota")`\unit{\percent}; control, `r custom_min_max(input = taxonomy_seawater_ctrl, taxon = "Thermoplasmatota")`\unit{\percent}). The sediment microbial community differed from the seawater community and was mainly composed of the bacterial taxa *Gammaproteobacteria* (aquaculture, `r custom_min_max(input = taxonomy_sediment_aq, taxon = "Gammaproteobacteria")`\unit{\percent}; control, `r custom_min_max(input = taxonomy_sediment_ctrl, taxon = "Gammaproteobacteria")`\unit{\percent}), *Thermodesulfobacteriota* (aquaculture, `r custom_min_max(input = taxonomy_sediment_aq, taxon = "Thermodesulfobacteriota")`\unit{\percent}; control, `r custom_min_max(input = taxonomy_sediment_ctrl, taxon = "Thermodesulfobacteriota")`\unit{\percent}), *Planctomycetota* (aquaculture, `r custom_min_max(input = taxonomy_sediment_aq, taxon = "Planctomycetota")`\unit{\percent}; control, `r custom_min_max(input = taxonomy_sediment_ctrl, taxon = "Planctomycetota")`\unit{\percent}), *Bacteroidota* (aquaculture, `r custom_min_max(input = taxonomy_sediment_aq, taxon = "Bacteroidota")`\unit{\percent}; control, `r custom_min_max(input = taxonomy_sediment_ctrl, taxon = "Bacteroidota")`\unit{\percent}), *Chloroflexota* (aquaculture, `r custom_min_max(input = taxonomy_sediment_aq, taxon = "Chloroflexota")`\unit{\percent}; control, `r custom_min_max(input = taxonomy_sediment_ctrl, taxon = "Chloroflexota")`\unit{\percent}), members without known relatives within *Bacteria* (aquaculture, `r custom_min_max(input = taxonomy_sediment_aq, taxon = "Bacteria_unclassified")`\unit{\percent}; control, `r custom_min_max(input = taxonomy_sediment_ctrl, taxon = "Bacteria_unclassified")`\unit{\percent}), and the archaeal phylum *Thermoproteota* (aquaculture, `r custom_min_max(input = taxonomy_sediment_aq, taxon = "Thermoproteota")`\unit{\percent}; control, `r custom_min_max(input = taxonomy_sediment_ctrl, taxon = "Thermoproteota")`\unit{\percent}; Fig. \@ref(fig:taxonomy-environments-sites)). In contrast to the taxonomic composition of both the seawater and sediment microbial communities, the gill microbiome was largely composed of the bacterial class *Gammaproteobacteria* (aquaculture, `r custom_min_max(input = taxonomy_gills_aq, taxon = "Gammaproteobacteria")`\unit{\percent}), while the proportions of other high-rank bacterial groups, such as *Alphaproteobacteria* (aquaculture, `r custom_min_max(input = taxonomy_gills_aq, taxon = "Alphaproteobacteria")`\unit{\percent}), *Bacteroidota* (aquaculture, `r custom_min_max(input = taxonomy_gills_aq, taxon = "Bacteroidota")`\unit{\percent}), *Planctomycetota* (aquaculture, `r custom_min_max(input = taxonomy_gills_aq, taxon = "Planctomycetota")`\unit{\percent}), *Bacillota* (aquaculture, `r custom_min_max(input = taxonomy_gills_aq, taxon = "Bacillota")`\unit{\percent}) or members without known relatives within *Bacteria* (aquaculture, `r custom_min_max(input = taxonomy_gills_aq, taxon = "Bacteria_unclassified")`\unit{\percent}), were substantially lower (Fig. \@ref(fig:taxonomy-environments-sites)). The high proportion of *Gammaproteobacteria* in the gill microbiome was observed in individuals at both locations and throughout the study period, while *Alphaproteobacteria*, *Bacteroidota*, and *Bacillota* comprised a notable proportion of the gill microbiome only in a few individuals (Supplementary Fig. \@ref(supp-fig:taxonomy-gills-phylum)).

```{r, results-taxonomy-seawater}

# Clean taxonomy data using the custom function
taxonomy_seawater <- custom_clean_taxonomy(
  input = taxonomy,
  control = c("PC1", "PC2", "NC1", "NC2"),
  remove_chloroplast = TRUE)

# Filter and structure the taxonomy data using the custom function
taxonomy_seawater <- custom_structure_taxonomy(
  input = taxonomy_seawater,
  metadata = metadata %>%
    # Customise metadata using the custom function
    format_labels() %>%
    # Remove positive and negative controls from metadata
    filter(!(id %in% c("PC1", "PC2", "NC1", "NC2"))),
  taxa_colour = NULL,
  select_environment = "Seawater",
  select_taxlevel = 2,
  threshold = 3,
  include_chloroplast = FALSE,
  split_pseudomonadota = TRUE)

# Group the samples by "location", "month", "year", and
# "taxon" and summarise the data by calculating the
# mean, standard deviation, and minimum and maximum values
taxonomy_seawater <-
  taxonomy_seawater$longer_metadata_taxonomy %>%
  group_by(location, month, year, taxon) %>%
  summarise(mean = mean(proportion),
            sd = sd(proportion),
            min = min(proportion),
            max = max(proportion),
            .groups = "drop")

# Extract the proportions of the taxa of interest for reporting
seawater_themoplasmatota_nov_2020_jan_2021 <- taxonomy_seawater %>%
  filter(taxon == "Thermoplasmatota") %>%
  filter((month == "November" & year == "2020") |
         (month == "January" & year == "2021"))
seawater_themoplasmatota_other <- taxonomy_seawater %>%
  filter(taxon == "Thermoplasmatota") %>%
  filter(!((month == "November" & year == "2020") |
           (month == "January" & year == "2021")))
seawater_gammaproteobacteria_lim_may_2021 <- taxonomy_seawater %>%
  filter(taxon == "Gammaproteobacteria") %>%
  filter(location == "Lim Bay" & (month == "May" & year == "2021"))
seawater_gammaproteobacteria_mali_ston_may_2021 <- taxonomy_seawater %>%
  filter(taxon == "Gammaproteobacteria") %>%
  filter(location == "Mali Ston Bay" & (month == "May" & year == "2021"))
seawater_gammaproteobacteria_other <- taxonomy_seawater %>%
  filter(taxon == "Gammaproteobacteria") %>%
  filter(!((location == "Lim Bay" & (month == "May" & year == "2021")) |
           (location == "Mali Ston Bay" & (month == "May" & year == "2021"))))
seawater_verrucomicrobiota_lim_may_2021 <- taxonomy_seawater %>%
  filter(taxon == "Verrucomicrobiota") %>%
  filter(location == "Lim Bay" & (month == "May" & year == "2021"))
seawater_verrucomicrobiota_mali_ston_may_2021 <- taxonomy_seawater %>%
  filter(taxon == "Verrucomicrobiota") %>%
  filter(location == "Mali Ston Bay" & (month == "May" & year == "2021"))
seawater_verrucomicrobiota_other <- taxonomy_seawater %>%
  filter(taxon == "Verrucomicrobiota") %>%
  filter(!((location == "Lim Bay" & (month == "May" & year == "2021")) |
           (location == "Mali Ston Bay" & (month == "May" & year == "2021"))))
seawater_alphaproteobacteria_lim_may_2021 <- taxonomy_seawater %>%
  filter(taxon == "Alphaproteobacteria") %>%
  filter(location == "Lim Bay" & (month == "May" & year == "2021"))
seawater_alphaproteobacteria_mali_ston_may_2021 <- taxonomy_seawater %>%
  filter(taxon == "Alphaproteobacteria") %>%
  filter(location == "Mali Ston Bay" & (month == "May" & year == "2021"))
seawater_alphaproteobacteria_other <- taxonomy_seawater %>%
  filter(taxon == "Alphaproteobacteria") %>%
  filter(!((location == "Lim Bay" & (month == "May" & year == "2021")) |
           (location == "Mali Ston Bay" & (month == "May" & year == "2021"))))

```

```{r, results-taxonomy-seawater-themoplasmatota}

# Filter samples of interest from the metadata
filtered_metadata <- metadata %>%
  # Customise metadata using the custom function
  format_labels() %>%
  # Filter the environment of interest
  filter(environment == "Seawater") %>%
  # Filter the sampling month and year of interest
  filter((month == "November" & year == "2020") |
         (month == "January" & year == "2021"))

# Structure the taxonomy data and calculate the proportion
# of the taxa of interest
marine_group_II <- taxonomy %>%
  # Select the samples of interest
  select(c(1 : total, all_of(x = filtered_metadata$id))) %>%
  # Filter the taxa of interest
  filter(taxon == "Thermoplasmatota" |
         taxon == "Marine_Group_II") %>%
  # Select columns containing taxa names and abundance data
  # of samples of interest
  select(taxon,  c(total : last_col()), -total) %>%
  # Transform the sequence abundance data into a tidy long format
  pivot_longer(cols = -taxon, names_to = "id",
               values_to = "seq_no") %>%
  # Transform the taxa names into a tidy wide format
  pivot_wider(names_from = taxon, values_from = seq_no) %>%
  # Group samples by sample IDs
  group_by(id) %>%
  # Calculate the proportion
  summarise(proportion = Marine_Group_II / Thermoplasmatota * 100)

```

```{r, results-taxonomy-sediment}

# Clean taxonomy data using the custom function
taxonomy_seawater <- custom_clean_taxonomy(
  input = taxonomy,
  control = c("PC1", "PC2", "NC1", "NC2"),
  remove_chloroplast = TRUE)

# Filter and structure the taxonomy data using the custom function
taxonomy_seawater <- custom_structure_taxonomy(
  input = taxonomy_seawater,
  metadata = metadata %>%
    # Customise metadata using the custom function
    format_labels() %>%
    # Remove positive and negative controls from metadata
    filter(!(id %in% c("PC1", "PC2", "NC1", "NC2"))),
  taxa_colour = NULL,
  select_environment = "Sediment",
  select_taxlevel = 2,
  threshold = 3,
  include_chloroplast = FALSE,
  split_pseudomonadota = TRUE)

# Extract the proportions of the taxa of interest for reporting
sediment_thermodesulfobacteriota_mali_ston_ctrl_2020 <-
  taxonomy_seawater$longer_metadata_taxonomy %>%
  filter(taxon == "Thermodesulfobacteriota") %>%
  filter(location == "Mali Ston Bay" & site == "Control") %>%
  filter(year == "2020")
sediment_thermodesulfobacteriota_mali_ston_ctrl_2021 <-
  taxonomy_seawater$longer_metadata_taxonomy %>%
  filter(taxon == "Thermodesulfobacteriota") %>%
  filter(location == "Mali Ston Bay" & site == "Control") %>%
  filter(year == "2021")
sediment_thermodesulfobacteriota_mali_ston_aq <-
  taxonomy_seawater$longer_metadata_taxonomy %>%
  filter(taxon == "Thermodesulfobacteriota") %>%
  filter(location == "Mali Ston Bay" & site == "Aquaculture")
sediment_thermodesulfobacteriota_lim <-
  taxonomy_seawater$longer_metadata_taxonomy %>%
  filter(taxon == "Thermodesulfobacteriota") %>%
  filter(location == "Lim Bay")

```
A more detailed analysis of the taxonomic composition of the seawater microbial community revealed temporal patterns for some taxa (Supplementary Fig. \@ref(supp-fig:taxonomy-seawater-sediment-phylum)). For example, the archaeal phylum *Thermoplasmatota* showed a similar pattern at both locations and sites, comprising a higher proportion of the community from November 2020 to January 2021 (`r min(seawater_themoplasmatota_nov_2020_jan_2021$min)`–`r max(seawater_themoplasmatota_nov_2020_jan_2021$max)`\unit{\percent}) than in other months (`r min(seawater_themoplasmatota_other$min)`–`r max(seawater_themoplasmatota_other$max)`\unit{\percent}). Notably, during the months when the proportion of this archaeal phylum was high, almost the entire *Thermoplasmatota* community consisted of Marine Group II (`r min(marine_group_II$proportion)`–`r max(marine_group_II$proportion)`\unit{\percent}). Marked differences between locations were observed in May 2021, when in Lim Bay at both sites, more than half of the microbial community was composed of *Gammaproteobacteria* (`r min(seawater_gammaproteobacteria_lim_may_2021$min)`–`r max(seawater_gammaproteobacteria_lim_may_2021$max)`\unit{\percent}) and *Verrucomicrobiota* (`r min(seawater_verrucomicrobiota_lim_may_2021$min)`–`r max(seawater_verrucomicrobiota_lim_may_2021$max)`\unit{\percent}), which was higher than in Mali Ston Bay at the same time (*Gammaproteobacteria*, `r min(seawater_gammaproteobacteria_mali_ston_may_2021$min)`–`r max(seawater_gammaproteobacteria_mali_ston_may_2021$max)`\unit{\percent}; *Verrucomicrobiota*, `r min(seawater_verrucomicrobiota_mali_ston_may_2021$min)`–`r max(seawater_verrucomicrobiota_mali_ston_may_2021$max)`\unit{\percent}) or in other months (*Gammaproteobacteria*, `r min(seawater_gammaproteobacteria_other$min)`–`r max(seawater_gammaproteobacteria_other$max)`\unit{\percent}; *Verrucomicrobiota*, `r min(seawater_verrucomicrobiota_other$min)`–`r max(seawater_verrucomicrobiota_other$max)`\unit{\percent}). In contrast, *Alphaproteobacteria* constituted a lower proportion of the microbial community in Lim Bay in May 2021 (`r min(seawater_alphaproteobacteria_lim_may_2021$min)`–`r max(seawater_alphaproteobacteria_lim_may_2021$max)`\unit{\percent}) than in Mali Ston Bay at the same time (`r min(seawater_alphaproteobacteria_mali_ston_may_2021$min)`–`r max(seawater_alphaproteobacteria_mali_ston_may_2021$max)`\unit{\percent}) or in other months (`r min(seawater_alphaproteobacteria_other$min)`–`r max(seawater_alphaproteobacteria_other$max)`\unit{\percent}). For the sediment microbial community, a fairly similar taxonomic composition between locations and sites, as well as across sampling months, was observed (Supplementary Fig. \@ref(supp-fig:taxonomy-seawater-sediment-phylum)). The only notable exception was the sediment community in Mali Ston Bay at the control site, where, for example, *Thermodesulfobacteriota* comprised a lower proportion of the community from July to November 2020 (`r min(sediment_thermodesulfobacteriota_mali_ston_ctrl_2020$proportion)`–`r max(sediment_thermodesulfobacteriota_mali_ston_ctrl_2020$proportion)`\unit{\percent}) than from January to May 2021 (`r min(sediment_thermodesulfobacteriota_mali_ston_ctrl_2021$proportion)`–`r max(sediment_thermodesulfobacteriota_mali_ston_ctrl_2021$proportion)`\unit{\percent}). This low proportion of *Thermodesulfobacteriota* was also lower than that observed at the aquaculture site in Mali Ston Bay (`r min(sediment_thermodesulfobacteriota_mali_ston_aq$proportion)`–`r max(sediment_thermodesulfobacteriota_mali_ston_aq$proportion)`\unit{\percent}) and at both sites in Lim Bay (`r min(sediment_thermodesulfobacteriota_lim$proportion)`–`r max(sediment_thermodesulfobacteriota_lim$proportion)`\unit{\percent}). 

```{r, taxonomy-gills-genus, out.width = "70%", fig.align = "center", fig.cap = "(ref:taxonomy-gills-genus)", fig.pos = "t!"}

include_graphics(path = "../results/figures/taxonomy_gills_genus.jpg")

```
(ref:taxonomy-gills-genus) Relative contribution of the most abundant (\qty{>=10}{\percent}) bacterial and archaeal sequences from the gills of the mussel *M. galloprovincialis* (gill microbiome) classified at the genus taxonomic rank. From July 2020 to May 2021, 10 mussels were collected per sampling month in Lim Bay and Mali Ston Bay. Each bar (individual index) represents an individual mussel. "No Relative" indicates sequences that could not be classified into any taxon, while "NR" (No Relative) next to the name of a taxonomic group refers to sequences that could not be assigned to any known taxon within that group. "NA" (Not Available) indicates samples that were not successfully analysed

```{r, results-taxonomy-gills}

# Clean taxonomy data using the custom function
taxonomy_gills <- custom_clean_taxonomy(
  input = taxonomy,
  control = c("PC1", "PC2", "NC1", "NC2"),
  remove_chloroplast = TRUE)

# Filter and structure the taxonomy data using the custom function
taxonomy_gills <- custom_structure_taxonomy(
  input = taxonomy_gills,
  metadata = metadata %>%
    # Customise metadata using the custom function
    format_labels() %>%
    # Remove positive and negative controls from metadata
    filter(!(id %in% c("PC1", "PC2", "NC1", "NC2"))),
  taxa_colour = NULL,
  select_environment = "Gills",
  select_taxlevel = 6,
  threshold = 10,
  include_chloroplast = FALSE,
  split_pseudomonadota = FALSE)

# Group the samples by "location", "month", "year", and
# "taxon" and summarise the data by calculating the
# mean, standard deviation, and minimum and maximum values
summarised_taxonomy_gills <-
  taxonomy_gills$longer_metadata_taxonomy %>%
  group_by(location, month, year, taxon) %>%
  summarise(mean = mean(proportion),
            sd = sd(proportion),
            min = min(proportion),
            max = max(proportion),
            .groups = "drop")

################################################################################
# Endozoicomonadaceae (NR)
################################################################################

# Extract the proportions of Endozoicomonadaceae (NR) for reporting
gills_endozoicomonadaceae_nr <- summarised_taxonomy_gills %>%
  filter(taxon == "Endozoicomonadaceae_unclassified")
gills_endozoicomonadaceae_nr_lim_sep_2020 <- summarised_taxonomy_gills %>%
  filter(taxon == "Endozoicomonadaceae_unclassified") %>%
  filter(location == "Lim Bay") %>%
  filter(month == "September" & year == "2020")
gills_endozoicomonadaceae_nr_mali_ston_sep_2020 <- summarised_taxonomy_gills %>%
  filter(taxon == "Endozoicomonadaceae_unclassified") %>%
  filter(location == "Mali Ston Bay") %>%
  filter(month == "September" & year == "2020")
gills_endozoicomonadaceae_nr_lim_jul_2020 <- summarised_taxonomy_gills %>%
  filter(taxon == "Endozoicomonadaceae_unclassified") %>%
  filter(location == "Lim Bay") %>%
  filter(month == "July" & year == "2020")
gills_endozoicomonadaceae_nr_lim_jan_2021 <- summarised_taxonomy_gills %>%
  filter(taxon == "Endozoicomonadaceae_unclassified") %>%
  filter(location == "Lim Bay") %>%
  filter(month == "January" & year == "2021")
gills_endozoicomonadaceae_nr_lim_mar_2021 <- summarised_taxonomy_gills %>%
  filter(taxon == "Endozoicomonadaceae_unclassified") %>%
  filter(location == "Lim Bay") %>%
  filter(month == "March" & year == "2021")

# Define the object name for storing statistical results
wilcox_gills_endozoicomonadaceae_nr_location <- NULL

# Test the differences between locations for each month using the
# Mann-Whitney U test
for (i in c("July_2020", "September_2020", "November_2020",
            "March_2021", "May_2021")) {
  
  # Prepare data for testing
  data <- taxonomy_gills$longer_metadata_taxonomy %>%
  # Filter the Endozoicomonadaceae (NR)
  filter(taxon == "Endozoicomonadaceae_unclassified") %>%
  # Define testing groups
  unite(col = group, month, year, sep = "_")
  
  # Filter the samples from each month from Lim Bay
  data_x <- data %>%
    filter(group == i) %>%
    filter(location == "Lim Bay")
  # Filter the samples from each month from Mali Ston Bay
  data_y <- data %>%
    filter(group == i) %>%
    filter(location == "Mali Ston Bay")
  
  # Perform the Mann-Whitney U test
  test <- wilcox.test(x = data_x$proportion, y = data_y$proportion,
                      alternative = "two.sided", paired = FALSE)
  
  # Store the statistical results in a tibble
  test_data <- tribble(
    # Define the column names for the tibble
    ~ month_year, ~ n_x, ~ n_y, ~ W, ~ p,
    # Add rows to the tibble with the Mann-Whitney U test results
    i, length(data_x$proportion), length(data_y$proportion),
    test$statistic, test$p.value)
  
  # Combine the results of each statistical test in a tibble
  wilcox_gills_endozoicomonadaceae_nr_location <-
    bind_rows(wilcox_gills_endozoicomonadaceae_nr_location,
              test_data)
  
}

# Define the object name for storing statistical results
kruskal_wallis_gills_endozoicomonadaceae_nr_months <- NULL
  
# Test differences between months for each location using the
# Kruskal-Wallis H test
for (i in c("Lim Bay", "Mali Ston Bay")) {
  
  # Prepare data for testing
  data <- taxonomy_gills$longer_metadata_taxonomy %>%
  # Filter the Endozoicomonadaceae (NR)
  filter(taxon == "Endozoicomonadaceae_unclassified") %>%
  # Remove samples from Mali Ston Bay from January 2021
  filter(!(location == "Mali Ston Bay" &
          month == "January" &
          year == "2021")) %>%
  # Define testing groups
  unite(col = group, month, year, sep = "_")
  
  # Filter each location
  data <- data %>%
    filter(location == i)
  
  # Perform the Kruskal-Wallis H test
  kruskal <- kruskal.test(formula = proportion ~ group,
                          data = data)
  
  # Perform the pairwise Mann-Whitney U test
  pairwise_wilcox <- pairwise.wilcox.test(
    x = data$proportion, g = data$group,
    p.adjust.method = "bonferroni")
  
  # Convert the results of the pairwise Mann-Whitney U test
  # to a tibble
  pairwise_wilcox <- pairwise_wilcox$p.value %>%
    # Convert the results to a tibble
    as_tibble(.name_repair = "check_unique", rownames = NA) %>%
    # Transfer the testing group names from row names to a column
    rownames_to_column(var = "rows") %>%
    # Transform the data into a tidy long format and remove rows
    # containing NA in the column defined by values_to
    pivot_longer(cols = -rows, names_to = "columns",
                 values_to = "p", values_drop_na = TRUE)
  
  # Store the statistical results in a tibble
  test_data <- tribble(
    # Define the column names for the tibble
    ~ location, ~ n, ~ kw_H, ~ kw_df, ~ kw_p, ~ mw_p,
```
