```{r, results-taxonomy-gills}
    # Add rows to the tibble with the Kruskal-Wallis H test and
    # pairwise Mann-Whitney U test results
    i, count(x = data, group),
    kruskal$statistic, kruskal$parameter, kruskal$p.value,
    pairwise_wilcox)
  
  # Combine the statistical results of each location in a tibble
  kruskal_wallis_gills_endozoicomonadaceae_nr_months <-
    bind_rows(kruskal_wallis_gills_endozoicomonadaceae_nr_months,
              test_data)
  
}

# Extract statistical values for reporting 
kruskal_wallis_gills_endozoicomonadaceae_nr_months <-
  kruskal_wallis_gills_endozoicomonadaceae_nr_months %>%
  # Filter rows containing statistical results for Lim Bay
  filter(location == "Lim Bay") %>%
  # Expand the list-column "mw_p" containing tibbles into rows and columns
  unnest(cols = mw_p)

################################################################################
# Endozoicomonas
################################################################################

# Extract the proportions of Endozoicomonas for reporting
gills_endozoicomonas_2020 <- summarised_taxonomy_gills %>%
  filter(taxon == "Endozoicomonas") %>%
  filter(year == "2020")
gills_endozoicomonas_2021 <- summarised_taxonomy_gills %>%
  filter(taxon == "Endozoicomonas") %>%
  filter(year == "2021")

# Filter the proportions of Endozoicomonas from 2020
data_x <- taxonomy_gills$longer_metadata_taxonomy %>%
  filter(taxon == "Endozoicomonas") %>%
  filter(year == "2020")
# Filter the proportions of Endozoicomonas from 2021
data_y <- taxonomy_gills$longer_metadata_taxonomy %>%
  filter(taxon == "Endozoicomonas") %>%
  filter(year == "2021")

# Perform the Mann-Whitney U test
wilcox_gills_endozoicomonas <-
  wilcox.test(x = data_x$proportion, y = data_y$proportion,
              alternative = "two.sided", paired = FALSE)

################################################################################
# "Candidatus Endoecteinascidia"
################################################################################

# Extract the proportions of "Candidatus Endoecteinascidia" for reporting
gills_candidatus_endoecteinascidia_jul_2020 <- summarised_taxonomy_gills %>%
  filter(taxon == "Candidatus_Endoecteinascidia") %>%
  filter(month == "July" & year == "2020")
gills_candidatus_endoecteinascidia_other <- summarised_taxonomy_gills %>%
  filter(taxon == "Candidatus_Endoecteinascidia") %>%
  filter(!(month == "July" & year == "2020"))

# Define the object name for storing statistical results
wilcox_gills_candidatus_endoecteinascidia <- NULL

# Test differences between July 2020 and all other months using
# the Mann-Whitney U test
for (i in c("September_2020", "November_2020", "January_2021",
            "March_2021", "May_2021")) {
  
  # Prepare data for testing
  data <- taxonomy_gills$longer_metadata_taxonomy %>%
  # Filter the "Candidatus Endoecteinascidia"
  filter(taxon == "Candidatus_Endoecteinascidia") %>%
  # Define testing groups
  unite(col = group, month, year, sep = "_")
  
  # Filter the samples from July 2020
  data_x <- data %>%
    filter(group == "July_2020")
  # Filter the samples from other months
  data_y <- data %>%
    filter(group == i)
  
  # Perform the Mann-Whitney U test
  test <- wilcox.test(x = data_x$proportion, y = data_y$proportion,
                      alternative = "two.sided", paired = FALSE)
  
  # Store the statistical results in a tibble
  test_data <- tribble(
    # Define the column names for the tibble
    ~ rows, ~ columns, ~ n_x, ~ n_y, ~ W, ~ p,
    # Add rows to the tibble with the Mann-Whitney U test results
    "July_2020", i,
    length(data_x$proportion),
    length(data_y$proportion),
    test$statistic, test$p.value)
  
  # Combine the results of each statistical test in a tibble
  wilcox_gills_candidatus_endoecteinascidia <-
    bind_rows(wilcox_gills_candidatus_endoecteinascidia,
              test_data)
  
}

# Adjust the p-values
wilcox_gills_candidatus_endoecteinascidia <-
  wilcox_gills_candidatus_endoecteinascidia %>%
  mutate(p.adj = p.adjust(p = p, method = "bonferroni"))

################################################################################
# Vibrio
################################################################################

# Extract the proportions of Vibrio for reporting
gills_vibrio_lim_sep_2020 <- summarised_taxonomy_gills %>%
  filter(taxon == "Vibrio") %>%
  filter(location == "Lim Bay") %>%
  filter(month == "September" & year == "2020")
gills_vibrio_mali_ston_sep_2020 <- summarised_taxonomy_gills %>%
  filter(taxon == "Vibrio") %>%
  filter(location == "Mali Ston Bay") %>%
  filter(month == "September" & year == "2020")
gills_vibrio_other <- summarised_taxonomy_gills %>%
  filter(taxon == "Vibrio") %>%
  filter(!(month == "September" & year == "2020"))

# Define the object name for storing statistical results
wilcox_gills_vibrio <- NULL

# Test differences between samples from Lim Bay from September 2020
# and samples from all other months and locations using the
# Mann-Whitney U test (samples from Mali Ston Bay from January 2021
# are excluded from testing)
for (i in c("Lim_Bay_July_2020", "Lim_Bay_November_2020",
            "Lim_Bay_January_2021", "Lim_Bay_March_2021",
            "Lim_Bay_May_2021", "Mali_Ston_Bay_July_2020",
            "Mali_Ston_Bay_September_2020", "Mali_Ston_Bay_November_2020",
            "Mali_Ston_Bay_March_2021", "Mali_Ston_Bay_May_2021")) {
  
  # Prepare data for testing
  data <- taxonomy_gills$longer_metadata_taxonomy %>%
  # Filter the Vibrio
  filter(taxon == "Vibrio") %>%
  # Define testing groups
  unite(col = group, location, month, year, sep = "_") %>%
  # Replace spaces with underscores in group names
  mutate(group = str_replace_all(string = group,
                                 pattern = " ",
                                 replacement = "_"))
  
  # Filter the samples from Lim Bay from September 2020
  data_x <- data %>%
    filter(group == "Lim_Bay_September_2020")
  # Filter the samples from other months and locations
  data_y <- data %>%
    filter(group == i)
  
  # Perform the Mann-Whitney U test
  test <- wilcox.test(x = data_x$proportion, y = data_y$proportion,
                      alternative = "two.sided", paired = FALSE)
  
  # Store the statistical results in a tibble
  test_data <- tribble(
    # Define the column names for the tibble
    ~ rows, ~ columns, ~ n_x, ~ n_y, ~ W, ~ p,
    # Add rows to the tibble with the Mann-Whitney U test results
    "Lim_Bay_September_2020", i,
    length(data_x$proportion),
    length(data_y$proportion),
    test$statistic, test$p.value)
  
  # Combine the results of each statistical test in a tibble
  wilcox_gills_vibrio <-
    bind_rows(wilcox_gills_vibrio,
              test_data)
  
}

# Adjust the p-values
wilcox_gills_vibrio <- wilcox_gills_vibrio %>%
  mutate(p.adj = p.adjust(p = p, method = "bonferroni"))

################################################################################
# Vibrionaceae (NR)
################################################################################

# Extract the proportions of Vibrionaceae (NR) for reporting
gills_vibrionaceae_nr_lim_sep_2020 <- summarised_taxonomy_gills %>%
  filter(taxon == "Vibrionaceae_unclassified") %>%
  filter(location == "Lim Bay") %>%
  filter(month == "September" & year == "2020")
gills_vibrionaceae_nr_lim_nov_2020 <- summarised_taxonomy_gills %>%
  filter(taxon == "Vibrionaceae_unclassified") %>%
  filter(location == "Lim Bay") %>%
  filter(month == "November" & year == "2020")
gills_vibrionaceae_nr_mali_ston_sep_2020 <- summarised_taxonomy_gills %>%
  filter(taxon == "Vibrionaceae_unclassified") %>%
  filter(location == "Mali Ston Bay") %>%
  filter(month == "September" & year == "2020")
gills_vibrionaceae_nr_mali_ston_nov_2020 <- summarised_taxonomy_gills %>%
  filter(taxon == "Vibrionaceae_unclassified") %>%
  filter(location == "Mali Ston Bay") %>%
  filter(month == "November" & year == "2020")
gills_vibrionaceae_nr_other <- summarised_taxonomy_gills %>%
  filter(taxon == "Vibrionaceae_unclassified") %>%
  filter(!((month == "September" & year == "2020") |
           (month == "November" & year == "2020")))

# Define the object name for storing statistical results
wilcox_gills_vibrionaceae_nr_lim_sep_2020 <- NULL

# Test differences between samples from Lim Bay from September 2020
# and samples from all other months and locations using the Mann-Whitney
# U test (samples from Mali Ston Bay from January 2021 are excluded from
# testing)
for (i in c("Lim_Bay_July_2020", "Lim_Bay_November_2020",
            "Lim_Bay_January_2021", "Lim_Bay_March_2021",
            "Lim_Bay_May_2021", "Mali_Ston_Bay_July_2020",
            "Mali_Ston_Bay_September_2020", "Mali_Ston_Bay_November_2020",
            "Mali_Ston_Bay_March_2021", "Mali_Ston_Bay_May_2021")) {
  
  # Prepare data for testing
  data <- taxonomy_gills$longer_metadata_taxonomy %>%
  # Filter the Vibrionaceae (NR)
  filter(taxon == "Vibrionaceae_unclassified") %>%
  # Define testing groups
  unite(col = group, location, month, year, sep = "_") %>%
  # Replace spaces with underscores in group names
  mutate(group = str_replace_all(string = group,
                                 pattern = " ",
                                 replacement = "_"))
  
  # Filter the samples from Lim Bay from September 2020
  data_x <- data %>%
    filter(group == "Lim_Bay_September_2020")
  # Filter the samples from other months and locations
  data_y <- data %>%
    filter(group == i)
  
  # Perform the Mann-Whitney U test
  test <- wilcox.test(x = data_x$proportion, y = data_y$proportion,
                      alternative = "two.sided", paired = FALSE)
  
  # Store the statistical results in a tibble
  test_data <- tribble(
    # Define the column names for the tibble
    ~ rows, ~ columns, ~ n_x, ~ n_y, ~ W, ~ p,
    # Add rows to the tibble with the Mann-Whitney U test results
    "Lim_Bay_September_2020", i,
    length(data_x$proportion),
    length(data_y$proportion),
    test$statistic, test$p.value)
  
  # Combine the results of each statistical test in a tibble
  wilcox_gills_vibrionaceae_nr_lim_sep_2020 <-
    bind_rows(wilcox_gills_vibrionaceae_nr_lim_sep_2020,
              test_data)
  
}

# Adjust the p-values
wilcox_gills_vibrionaceae_nr_lim_sep_2020 <-
  wilcox_gills_vibrionaceae_nr_lim_sep_2020 %>%
  mutate(p.adj = p.adjust(p = p, method = "bonferroni"))

# Define the object name for storing statistical results
wilcox_gills_vibrionaceae_nr_lim_nov_2020 <- NULL

# Test differences between samples from Lim Bay from November 2020
# and samples from all other months and locations using the Mann-Whitney
# U test (samples from Mali Ston Bay from January 2021 are excluded from
# testing)
for (i in c("Lim_Bay_July_2020", "Lim_Bay_September_2020",
            "Lim_Bay_January_2021", "Lim_Bay_March_2021",
            "Lim_Bay_May_2021", "Mali_Ston_Bay_July_2020",
            "Mali_Ston_Bay_September_2020", "Mali_Ston_Bay_November_2020",
            "Mali_Ston_Bay_March_2021", "Mali_Ston_Bay_May_2021")) {
  
  # Prepare data for testing
  data <- taxonomy_gills$longer_metadata_taxonomy %>%
  # Filter the Vibrionaceae (NR)
  filter(taxon == "Vibrionaceae_unclassified") %>%
  # Define testing groups
  unite(col = group, location, month, year, sep = "_") %>%
  # Replace spaces with underscores in group names
  mutate(group = str_replace_all(string = group,
                                 pattern = " ",
                                 replacement = "_"))
  
  # Filter the samples from Lim Bay from November 2020
  data_x <- data %>%
    filter(group == "Lim_Bay_November_2020")
  # Filter the samples from other months and locations
  data_y <- data %>%
    filter(group == i)
  
  # Perform the Mann-Whitney U test
  test <- wilcox.test(x = data_x$proportion, y = data_y$proportion,
                      alternative = "two.sided", paired = FALSE)
  
  # Store the statistical results in a tibble
  test_data <- tribble(
    # Define the column names for the tibble
    ~ rows, ~ columns, ~ n_x, ~ n_y, ~ W, ~ p,
    # Add rows to the tibble with the Mann-Whitney U test results
    "Lim_Bay_November_2020", i,
    length(data_x$proportion),
    length(data_y$proportion),
    test$statistic, test$p.value)
  
  # Combine the results of each statistical test in a tibble
  wilcox_gills_vibrionaceae_nr_lim_nov_2020 <-
    bind_rows(wilcox_gills_vibrionaceae_nr_lim_nov_2020,
              test_data)
  
}

# Adjust the p-values
wilcox_gills_vibrionaceae_nr_lim_nov_2020 <-
  wilcox_gills_vibrionaceae_nr_lim_nov_2020 %>%
  mutate(p.adj = p.adjust(p = p, method = "bonferroni"))

# Combine the results of each statistical test in a tibble
# (Lim Bay, September 2020 and Lim Bay, November 2020)
wilcox_gills_vibrionaceae_nr <-
  bind_rows(wilcox_gills_vibrionaceae_nr_lim_sep_2020,
            wilcox_gills_vibrionaceae_nr_lim_nov_2020)
```
Because, as previously mentioned, the gill microbiome of most *M. galloprovincialis* mussels, except for a few individuals, was largely composed of *Gammaproteobacteria*, no differences in community composition between locations or sampling months were identified (Supplementary Fig. \@ref(supp-fig:taxonomy-gills-phylum)). However, when the taxonomic composition was analysed at lower taxonomic ranks, such as family and genus, differences between locations and sampling months were observed (Fig. \@ref(fig:taxonomy-gills-genus)). Although high variability in the taxonomic composition of each individual's gill microbiome was noted, low-rank gammaproteobacterial groups present at both locations and throughout the study period, as well as groups specific to certain locations or months, were identified. Members without known relatives within *Endozoicomonadaceae* were present in mussel gills at both locations and throughout the study period, showing a high degree of variability between individuals (`r min(gills_endozoicomonadaceae_nr$min)`–`r max(gills_endozoicomonadaceae_nr$max)`\unit{\percent}; Fig. \@ref(fig:taxonomy-gills-genus)). When differences in the proportion of this group between individuals from different locations were tested, the only significant difference was its higher proportion in the gill microbiome of individuals from Mali Ston Bay (`r gills_endozoicomonadaceae_nr_mali_ston_sep_2020$min`–`r gills_endozoicomonadaceae_nr_mali_ston_sep_2020$max`\unit{\percent}) compared to those from Lim Bay (`r gills_endozoicomonadaceae_nr_lim_sep_2020$min`–`r gills_endozoicomonadaceae_nr_lim_sep_2020$max`\unit{\percent}) in September 2020 (Mann–Whitney *U* test, *p* `r filter(.data = wilcox_gills_endozoicomonadaceae_nr_location, month_year == "September_2020")$p %>% format_p_values(space = TRUE)`). In addition, significant differences between months were observed only in Lim Bay (Kruskal–Wallis *H* test, *p* `r format_p_values(x = max(kruskal_wallis_gills_endozoicomonadaceae_nr_months$kw_p), space = TRUE)`), specifically between July 2020 (`r gills_endozoicomonadaceae_nr_lim_jul_2020$min`–`r gills_endozoicomonadaceae_nr_lim_jul_2020$max`\unit{\percent}) and January 2021 (`r gills_endozoicomonadaceae_nr_lim_jan_2021$min`–`r gills_endozoicomonadaceae_nr_lim_jan_2021$max`\unit{\percent}), July 2020 and March 2021 (`r gills_endozoicomonadaceae_nr_lim_mar_2021$min`–`r gills_endozoicomonadaceae_nr_lim_mar_2021$max`\unit{\percent}), and between September 2020 (`r gills_endozoicomonadaceae_nr_lim_sep_2020$min`–`r gills_endozoicomonadaceae_nr_lim_sep_2020$max`\unit{\percent}) and January 2021 (pairwise Mann–Whitney *U* test, all stated comparisons *p* `r filter(.data = kruskal_wallis_gills_endozoicomonadaceae_nr_months, p < 0.05)$p %>% max() %>% format_p_values(space = TRUE)`). Interestingly, *Endozoicomonas*, a member of the family *Endozoicomonadaceae*, was almost absent in the gill microbiome at both locations from July to November 2020 (`r min(gills_endozoicomonas_2020$min)`–`r max(gills_endozoicomonas_2020$max)`\unit{\percent}), but from January to May 2021 it comprised a substantial proportion of the microbiome in many individuals from both locations (`r min(gills_endozoicomonas_2021$min)`–`r max(gills_endozoicomonas_2021$max)`\unit{\percent}; Mann–Whitney *U* test, *p* `r format_p_values(x = wilcox_gills_endozoicomonas$p.value, space = TRUE)`; Fig. \@ref(fig:taxonomy-gills-genus)). Another taxonomic group showing specificity to a certain period was "*Candidatus* Endoecteinascidia", which comprised a substantial proportion of the gill microbiome in many individuals in July 2020 (`r min(gills_candidatus_endoecteinascidia_jul_2020$min)`–`r max(gills_candidatus_endoecteinascidia_jul_2020$max)`\unit{\percent}), while in other months the microbiomes of only a few individuals contained a higher proportion of this group (`r min(gills_candidatus_endoecteinascidia_other$min)`–`r max(gills_candidatus_endoecteinascidia_other$max)`\unit{\percent}; Fig. \@ref(fig:taxonomy-gills-genus)). These differences in proportions of "*Candidatus* Endoecteinascidia" between July 2020 and all other months were supported by the Mann–Whitney *U* test (all *p* `r format_p_values(x = max(wilcox_gills_candidatus_endoecteinascidia$p.adj), space = TRUE)`). *Vibrio* showed differences in the proportion of the gill microbiome between locations and sampling months (Fig. \@ref(fig:taxonomy-gills-genus)). In September 2020, higher proportions of this group were observed in gill microbiomes from Lim Bay (`r gills_vibrio_lim_sep_2020$min`–`r gills_vibrio_lim_sep_2020$max`\unit{\percent}) than from Mali Ston Bay (`r gills_vibrio_mali_ston_sep_2020$min`–`r gills_vibrio_mali_ston_sep_2020$max`\unit{\percent}). In addition, these proportions in September 2020 were also higher than those observed in all other months at either location (`r min(gills_vibrio_other$min)`–`r max(gills_vibrio_other$max)`\unit{\percent}). These differences in the proportion of the gill microbiome between individuals from Lim Bay in September 2020 and individuals from all other months at either location were supported by the Mann–Whitney *U* test (all *p* `r format_p_values(x = max(wilcox_gills_vibrio$p.adj), space = TRUE)`; samples from Mali Ston Bay from January 2021 were excluded due to the limited number of samples). Furthermore, 
members without known relatives within *Vibrionaceae* also showed differences in the proportion of the gill microbiome between locations and sampling months (Fig. \@ref(fig:taxonomy-gills-genus)). Higher proportions of this group were observed in individuals from Lim Bay in September (`r gills_vibrionaceae_nr_lim_sep_2020$min`–`r gills_vibrionaceae_nr_lim_sep_2020$max`\unit{\percent}) and November (`r gills_vibrionaceae_nr_lim_nov_2020$min`–`r gills_vibrionaceae_nr_lim_nov_2020$max`\unit{\percent}) 2020 than in individuals from Mali Ston Bay in the same months (September 2020, `r gills_vibrionaceae_nr_mali_ston_sep_2020$min`–`r gills_vibrionaceae_nr_mali_ston_sep_2020$max`\unit{\percent}; November 2020, `r gills_vibrionaceae_nr_mali_ston_nov_2020$min`–`r gills_vibrionaceae_nr_mali_ston_nov_2020$max`\unit{\percent}). In addition, these proportions in individuals from Lim Bay from September to November 2020 were also higher than those observed in individuals from all other months at either location (`r min(gills_vibrionaceae_nr_other$min)`–`r max(gills_vibrionaceae_nr_other$max)`\unit{\percent}). The similarities in proportions of this group in individuals from Lim Bay in September and November 2020 and the differences from individuals from all other months at either location were supported by the Mann–Whitney *U* test (all *p* `r filter(.data = wilcox_gills_vibrionaceae_nr, columns != "Lim_Bay_September_2020" & columns != "Lim_Bay_November_2020")$p.adj %>% max() %>% format_p_values(space = TRUE)`; samples from Mali Ston Bay from January 2021 were excluded due to the limited number of samples).
\newpage

# Discussion

The mussel *M. galloprovincialis* is a filter-feeding animal, meaning that, as in most bivalve species, its gills are used for both gas exchange and feeding [@Gosling2003a]. As the gills are in constant contact with seawater and may be influenced by sediment resuspension, it is advisable to compare the gill microbiome with the microbial communities in the surrounding seawater and sediment. In this study, the observed number of OTUs, as well as the exponential of the Shannon diversity index and the Inverse Simpson diversity index, were significantly lower for the gill microbiome than for the seawater and sediment microbial communities. However, when the total number of OTUs is considered using calculated richness estimators, the difference in the number of OTUs between the gill microbiome and the seawater microbial community is no longer evident. It appears that the alpha diversity of the *M. galloprovincialis* gill microbiome is similar to that of the microbial community in ambient seawater, or at least the differences are not substantial. A study on the gill microbiome of the same species reported differences in alpha diversity between the gill microbiome and the microbial community in the surrounding seawater [@Zardinoni2024], while another study found no differences between the microbial community in the surrounding seawater and the microbiome of various body parts of this species, including the gills [@Musella2020]. In any case, the alpha diversity of the gill microbiome and seawater microbial community was substantially lower than that of the sediment community. Similarly, comparisons of the gut and shell microbiome of the eastern oyster, *Crassostrea virginica*, with the sediment microbial community also found lower alpha diversity in the oyster's gut and shell microbiome than in the sediment community [@Arfken2017]. The alpha diversity of the gill microbiome did not differ between the two locations, Lim Bay and Mali Ston Bay, consistent with a study comparing the gill microbiome of *M. galloprovincialis* between nearby locations [@Zardinoni2024], as well as another study comparing its whole microbiome between more distant geographic locations [@Peruzzo2026]. Richness estimators were also the only alpha diversity parameters showing significant differences in the gill microbiome between sampling months, although the only significant difference was between January and March 2021. Overall, it appears that the mussel *M. galloprovincialis* has a gill microbiome with lower alpha diversity than the surrounding sediment microbial community and minimal difference from the community in the surrounding seawater. In addition, its gill microbiome appears to be stable in terms of alpha diversity, showing minimal variation between different sampling locations and months.

Although alpha diversity parameters did not indicate a strong difference between the gill microbiome of *M. galloprovincialis* and the microbial community in the surrounding seawater, Bray–Curtis dissimilarity calculated from OTU abundances revealed a clear separation between the gill microbiome and the communities in both the surrounding seawater and sediment. This finding, that the mussel *M. galloprovincialis* harbours a gill microbiome with a community structure distinct from that of the microbial community in ambient seawater, is consistent with studies reporting differences between the microbiome of this species' various body parts, such as the gut, gills, and haemolymph, and the community in the surrounding seawater [@Akter2023; @Musella2020; @Palladino2023; @Vezzulli2018; @Zardinoni2024]. Furthermore, the difference between the gill microbiome of this species and the sediment microbial community is similar to the observed distinction in community structure between the gut and shell microbiome of the eastern oyster and the microbial community in the surrounding sediment [@Arfken2017]. Comparison of the overall structure of the *M. galloprovincialis* gill microbiome at the OTU level in this study did not reveal significant differentiation between the two locations, Lim Bay and Mali Ston Bay, despite their considerable geographic distance. This agrees with a study of the gill microbiome of the same species that also did not show variation between locations [@Zardinoni2024]. In contrast, the structure of the gut microbiome of the same species varied with geographic location [@delRio-Lavin2023; @Palladino2023], indicating a greater influence of local conditions on the gut than on the gill microbiome, possibly due to variation in the origin of food, which also includes microbes, with geographic location and the potential involvement of the gut microbiota in food digestion [@Gosling2003c; @Pierce2018]. Interestingly, the structure of the overall microbiome of the mussel *M. galloprovincialis* transported from one geographic location to another is initially different and converges towards that of native mussels, highlighting the influence of local conditions, the possible high contribution of the gut microbiota to the overall microbiome, and the importance of assessing tissue-specific microbiomes [@Peruzzo2026]. Furthermore, in this study, the structure of the gill microbiome at the OTU level showed a separation of microbiomes from July 2020, from September 2020 to January 2021, and from March to May 2021, indicating the presence of microbiome taxa with temporal changes. Similarly, studies on the overall microbiome of the mussel *M. galloprovincialis* [@Peruzzo2026], as well as on the gut microbiome [@Akter2023; @Palladino2023; @Wathsala2021], also indicated temporal microbiome changes. In contrast, a study of the gill microbiome of the same species did not reveal any temporal changes, possibly due to the fewer sampling months included in that study [@Zardinoni2024].

Consistent with the Bray–Curtis dissimilarities calculated from OTU abundances, the taxonomic analysis also revealed a clear distinction between the gill microbiome of the mussel *M. galloprovincialis* and the communities in the surrounding seawater and sediment. The high-rank microbial taxa detected in the surrounding seawater, as well as the higher proportion of *Thermoplasmatota* Marine Group II during the colder period of the year, are typical of seawater microbial communities in the Adriatic Sea [@Korlevic2015; @Korlevic2022]. Similarly, the high-rank microbial taxa detected in the surrounding sediment have previously been found in surface sediments of the Adriatic Sea [@Markovski2022; @Ramljak2024]. At higher taxonomic ranks, the distinction between the gill microbiome and the microbial communities in the surrounding seawater and sediment was mainly due to the high proportion of *Gammaproteobacteria* in the gill microbiome. The high proportion of taxa belonging to this class in the gill microbiome has also been observed in other studies focusing on the gills of the mussel *M. galloprovincialis* [@Musella2020; @Zardinoni2024]. Because of the high proportion of *Gammaproteobacteria* in the gill microbiome, no differences between the two locations, Lim Bay and Mali Ston Bay, or between sampling months were observed. However, when the analysis was performed at lower taxonomic ranks, such as family and genus, both resident taxa, present at both locations and throughout the study period, and transient taxa, specific to certain locations or months, were identified.

Although members without known relatives within *Endozoicomonadaceae* showed high variability in their proportion within the gill microbiome of *M. galloprovincialis* between individuals, they were detected in all analysed mussels and exhibited minimal differences in proportion between mussels from different locations or months. This indicates that this group is resident in the gills of *M. galloprovincialis*. The family *Endozoicomonadaceae* is known to form associations with many marine benthic invertebrates, including bivalves, and with some fish [@daSilva2025; @Munoz-Martinez2026; @Schill2017]. Furthermore, members of this family have been detected in the gills of the blue mussel, *Mytilus edulis*, a bivalve species closely related to *M. galloprovincialis* [@Schill2017; @Kijewski2011]. Genomic analysis suggests that members of this family can rapidly adapt and transition between free-living and host-associated lifestyles, resulting in host–microbe relationships that can range from mutualistic to parasitic depending on the host species and ecological context [@daSilva2025]. The presence of this group in all analysed mussels in the present study suggests a mutualistic interaction between its members and *M. galloprovincialis*. However, as this group consists of sequences without currently known relatives within this family, it is possible that novel *Endozoicomonadaceae* taxa exhibiting temporal and spatial dynamics and showing different interactions with this host species will be described in the future. Indeed, in the present study, *Endozoicomonas*, the type genus for this family [@Kurahashi2007], was almost absent from the gill microbiome at both locations from July to November 2020. However, from January to May 2021, it comprised a substantial proportion of the microbiome in many individuals from both locations, indicating the transient nature of this taxon and possibly a different host–microbe interaction. Similarly, "*Candidatus* Endoecteinascidia" comprised a substantial proportion of the gill microbiome in many mussels from both locations in July 2020, while in other months, only a few individuals' microbiomes contained a higher proportion of this group, indicating its transient nature. This group has been described as an endosymbiont of the ascidian *Ecteinascidia turbinata* [@Moss2003]. Interestingly, genome analysis revealed an extreme reduction of the genome in this group and the presence of biosynthetic genes required for the production of ecteinascidin 743 (ET-743), a chemotherapeutic natural product thought to be pivotal for the interaction with this tunicate host [@Kehr2015; @Rath2011; @Schofield2015]. These data suggest that members of this group form a mutualistic relationship with the host, so at present, it is difficult to explain the observed transient nature of "*Candidatus* Endoecteinascidia" in the gill microbiome of *M. galloprovincialis* observed in this study. It is possible that the host of this group is not the mussel but an ascidian biofouling species, and its detection is due to the proximity of the ascidians to the mussel gills. Indeed, ascidians are important members of the biofouling community on various aquaculture bivalve species, including *M. galloprovincialis* [@Alvanou2024], and their presence was also observed in aquaculture farms in the northern Adriatic Sea, including Lim Bay [@Majnaric2022]. In addition, ascidians in the Mediterranean Sea, including the genus *Ecteinascidia*, have shown marked seasonality of growth on artificial plates deployed next to a bivalve aquaculture facility [@Casso2018], which could explain the observed higher proportion of its endosymbiont "*Candidatus* Endoecteinascidia" in the gill microbiome during the summer peak in July 2020 compared to other sampling months.

Groups that were transient in nature and also differed between the two aquaculture locations, Lim Bay and Mali Ston Bay, both belonged to the family *Vibrionaceae* and included *Vibrio* as well as members without known relatives within *Vibrionaceae*. Members of the family *Vibrionaceae* are often found in the microbiome of various bivalve species, including *M. galloprovincialis* [@delRio-Lavin2023; @Munoz-Martinez2026; @Musella2020; @Peruzzo2026; @Vezzulli2018], forming relationships with their bivalve hosts that can range from mutualistic to neutral or parasitic [@Destoumieux-Garzon2020]. The preference of *Vibrio* and members without known relatives within *Vibrionaceae* for the period from September to November 2020, as well as for the aquaculture location in Lim Bay, observed in this study, suggests a neutral or parasitic interaction with *M. galloprovincialis*. The higher proportion of *Vibrionaceae* observed during this period is consistent with a study using culture-dependent methods focusing on the gills of *M. galloprovincialis*, sampled during the same period and at the same aquaculture locations, which revealed a positive correlation between total *Vibrio* counts and temperature (L. Perić, personal communication). This is not surprising, as many species of *Vibrio* are known to thrive at higher temperatures [@Destoumieux-Garzon2020; @Sheikh2022]. The observed difference between Lim Bay and Mali Ston Bay could be due to distinct hydrological conditions and productivity at these locations, resulting in greater accumulation of organic matter and microbial growth in Lim Bay [@Bosak2013; @Matek2024; @Silovic2012a; @Skejic2015]. Indeed, all described *Vibrio* spp. are obligate heterotrophs that rely on organic matter as a carbon source [@Zhang2018a].

# Conclusions

Because studies assessing the microbiome of bivalve species, including the mussel *M. galloprovincialis*, have focused on gut tissue [@Akter2023; @delRio-Lavin2023; @Palladino2023; @Salloum2025; @Vezzulli2018; @Wathsala2021], knowledge of the temporal and spatial dynamics of microbiomes from other body parts of this important aquaculture species remains limited. This study analysed a comprehensive set of gill microbiomes and compared them with microbial communities in the surrounding seawater and sediment to address this gap. Overall, the structure of the gill microbiome of *M. galloprovincialis* at the OTU level was distinct from the microbial communities in the surrounding seawater and sediment, and did not differ between the aquaculture locations, Lim Bay and Mali Ston Bay. However, the structure of the gill microbiome varied between sampling months, suggesting temporal dynamics. Furthermore, taxonomic analysis revealed the presence of both resident and transient microbial taxa in the gill microbiome. Members without known relatives within *Endozoicomonadaceae* were observed in the gill microbiome of mussels from both locations and throughout the study period, indicating their resident nature. In contrast, *Endozoicomonas*, "*Candidatus* Endoecteinascidia", *Vibrio*, and members without known relatives within *Vibrionaceae* appeared to be transient, as they were observed only in certain months. To clarify the nature of the interaction between these taxa and the *M. galloprovincialis* host, future studies using methods that allow microbial cell tissue localisation and functional characterisation are required.
\newpage

# Acknowledgements

We thank the Department of Applied Ecology, University of Dubrovnik, and the Centre for Marine Research, Ruđer Bošković Institute, for providing access to laboratory facilities. We also thank the operator of the R/V Burin and the staff of Istrida d. o. o. for their cooperation and assistance with sampling.

# Author contributions

MK and LP conceived and designed the study. LP managed the project and secured funding. DK, IVS, KO, VK, JB, SBČ, and VN conducted fieldwork and collected samples. MK and MM processed the samples and performed molecular analyses. MK conducted the bioinformatic analyses and statistical tests, and wrote the first draft of the manuscript. All authors reviewed and approved the final manuscript.

# Funding

This study was funded by the Croatian Science Foundation through the BEST ADRIA project (project number IP-2019-04-1956) and the Young Researchers' Career Development Project—Training New Doctoral Students (project number DOK-2021-02-7567, awarded to LP for KO).

# Data availability

Nucleotide sequences of the V4 region of the 16S rRNA gene obtained in this study have been deposited in the European Nucleotide Archive at EMBL-EBI under accession number PRJEB107277 (<https://www.ebi.ac.uk/ena/browser/view/PRJEB107277>). The detailed analysis procedure, including the R Markdown file for this article, is available on GitHub (<https://github.com/MicrobesRovinj/Korlevic_MusselMicrobiome_MicrobEcol_2026>) and Zenodo (<https://doi.org>).
